title: Strong Sentences
blurb: The building block of all writing. Make every sentence clear and complete.

slides.html:
A sentence is a group of words that expresses one complete thought. Every proper sentence has two parts:

  • a subject — who or what the sentence is about
  • a verb — what the subject does or is
CompleteAma sells oranges at the market.
Incomplete (fragment)Selling oranges at the market.
RememberIf a group of words cannot stand alone and make sense, it is a fragment — not a sentence.
slides.say: A sentence is a group of words that expresses one complete thought. Every proper sentence has two parts. A subject, which is who or what the sentence is about. And a verb, which is what the subject does or is. For example: Ama sells oranges at the market. That is complete. But, selling oranges at the market, on its own, is a fragment because it is missing a subject and does not express a full thought.
slides.example: Ama sells oranges at the market every morning.

slides.html:
Two habits make sentences look professional immediately:

  • Begin with a capital letter.
  • End with a full stop, question mark, or exclamation mark.
Weakthe rain started early today
CorrectThe rain started early today.
TipRead your sentence aloud. Where your voice naturally stops, you usually need a full stop.
slides.say: Two habits make sentences look professional immediately. Begin every sentence with a capital letter, and end it with a full stop, a question mark, or an exclamation mark. Compare: the rain started early today, with no capital and no full stop, against, The rain started early today, written correctly. A good tip is to read your sentence aloud. Where your voice naturally stops, you usually need a full stop.
slides.example: The rain started early today.

slides.html:
A simple sentence has one subject and one verb. It expresses one idea.

Kofi plays football.
The teacher marked our books.

Simple sentences are powerful because they are clear. Use them when an idea must land strongly.
slides.say: A simple sentence has one subject and one verb, and expresses one idea. For example: Kofi plays football. Or: The teacher marked our books. Simple sentences are powerful because they are clear. Use them when you want an idea to land strongly.
slides.example: Kofi plays football after school.

slides.html:
A compound sentence joins two complete ideas using a joining word such as and, but, or, so.

Ama studied hard, so she passed the exam.
I wanted to go out, but it was raining.
PunctuationPut a comma before and, but, or, so when it joins two full sentences.
slides.say: A compound sentence joins two complete ideas using a joining word such as and, but, or, or so. For example: Ama studied hard, so she passed the exam. Or: I wanted to go out, but it was raining. One punctuation rule: put a comma before and, but, or, or so, when it joins two full sentences.
slides.example: Ama studied hard, so she passed the exam.

slides.html:
A complex sentence adds extra information using words like because, although, when, if, while.

Because the road was bad, the bus arrived late.
She smiled when she saw her results.

Mixing simple, compound and complex sentences makes your writing flow and sound mature.
slides.say: A complex sentence adds extra information using words like because, although, when, if, and while. For example: Because the road was bad, the bus arrived late. Or: She smiled when she saw her results. Mixing simple, compound, and complex sentences makes your writing flow and sound mature.
slides.example: Because the road was bad, the bus arrived late.

slides.html:
A run-on crams too many ideas together without proper punctuation or joining words.

Run-onI woke up late I missed the bus I was punished.
FixedI woke up late, so I missed the bus. As a result, I was punished.
Fix itBreak the idea into separate sentences, or join them properly with a comma and a joining word.
slides.say: A run-on sentence crams too many ideas together without proper punctuation or joining words. For example: I woke up late I missed the bus I was punished. That is a run-on. Fixed, it reads: I woke up late, so I missed the bus. As a result, I was punished. To fix a run-on, break the idea into separate sentences, or join them properly with a comma and a joining word.
slides.example: I woke up late, so I missed the bus and was punished.

slides.html:
The verb must match the subject. One thing takes a singular verb; many things take a plural verb.

The students was happy.
The students were happy.
The student was happy.
Quick checkFind the real subject, then ask: one or many?
slides.say: The verb must match the subject. One thing takes a singular verb, while many things take a plural verb. For example, the students was happy, is wrong. The students were happy, is correct, because students is plural. But, the student was happy, is correct, because there is only one student. A quick check: find the real subject, then ask yourself, one, or many?
slides.example: The students were happy when the results came.

slides.html:
By their purpose, sentences come in four kinds:

  • Statement — gives information. School begins at eight.
  • Question — asks something. What time does school begin?
  • Command — gives an instruction. Close the door.
  • Exclamation — shows strong feeling. What a goal that was!
Why it mattersMixing these four kinds keeps your writing lively instead of flat.
slides.say: By their purpose, sentences come in four kinds. A statement gives information, such as, school begins at eight. A question asks something, such as, what time does school begin? A command gives an instruction, such as, close the door. And an exclamation shows strong feeling, such as, what a goal that was! Mixing these four kinds keeps your writing lively, instead of flat.
slides.example: Please close the door. Did you finish your homework? What a beautiful morning! The market opens at six.

slides.html:
Small marks guide your reader. The most useful are:

  • Comma (,) — separates items in a list and marks short pauses. I bought rice, beans, oil and salt.
  • Apostrophe (') — shows ownership or shortens words. Ama's book; it's raining.
  • Question mark (?) — ends a question.
Watch outDo not scatter commas randomly. Use one only where the reader should pause.
slides.say: Small marks guide your reader. The most useful are these. The comma separates items in a list and marks short pauses, for example, I bought rice, beans, oil and salt. The apostrophe shows ownership, or shortens words, for example, Ama's book, or, it's raining. And the question mark ends a question. One warning. Do not scatter commas randomly. Use one only where the reader should pause.
slides.example: After the rain stopped, we swept the yard, washed the pots, and cooked supper.

slides.html:
Before you move on, every sentence you write should pass this check:

  • Does it have a subject and a verb?
  • Does it express one complete thought?
  • Capital letter at the start, punctuation at the end?
  • Does the verb agree with the subject?
  • Is it free of run-ons?
You did itStrong sentences are the foundation of strong paragraphs. Try the Practice tab next!
slides.say: Before you move on, every sentence you write should pass this checklist. Does it have a subject and a verb? Does it express one complete thought? Does it start with a capital letter and end with punctuation? Does the verb agree with the subject? And is it free of run-ons? Strong sentences are the foundation of strong paragraphs. Well done. Try the Practice tab next.
slides.example: A strong sentence has a subject and a verb, begins with a capital letter, and ends with the right punctuation.

rewrite.weak: the market was crowded people pushing everywhere i could not find my mother
rewrite.hint: Break the run-on into clear sentences. Add capitals and full stops.
rewrite.model: The market was crowded. People were pushing everywhere, so I could not find my mother.
rubric: Each sentence has a subject and a verb
Capital letters and full stops are used
The run-on is broken into clear ideas
A joining word (so/and/but) is used naturally

rewrite.weak: my brother and me was going to school we saw a accident
rewrite.hint: Fix the pronoun, the agreement, and the run-on.
rewrite.model: My brother and I were going to school when we saw an accident.
rubric: 'I' is used instead of 'me' as the subject
The verb 'were' agrees with the plural subject
The two ideas are joined smoothly with 'when'

points.passage: Kwame is a hard-working student. He wakes up at five o'clock every morning to study before school. After lessons, he helps his mother sell at the market until evening. Even when he is tired, he still finds time to revise his notes at night.
model: Kwame is hard-working / disciplined
He studies early in the morning before school
He helps his mother at the market after school
He revises at night even when tired

points: Lost his way in a big town
frompoints.instruction: Write 3–4 connected sentences telling this little story. Use at least one compound and one complex sentence.
frompoints.model: A boy named Yaw lost his way in a big town. Because he did not know the streets, he became frightened. Then a kind woman saw him crying, and she helped him find the bus station. Finally, Yaw reached home safely and thanked her.
rubric: All four points are included in order

question.q: Write FIVE clear sentences describing your school. Use a simple, a compound, and a complex sentence among them.
question.model: My school is called Unity Senior High School. It is large, and it has many classrooms. Although the buildings are old, they are well kept. The teachers are friendly because they care about us. I am proud to be a student there.

title: Features of a Good Paragraph
blurb: What a paragraph is, its parts, and the four marks of a strong one: unity, coherency, completeness, consistency.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Explain what a paragraph is.
  • Name the three parts of a paragraph.
  • Identify and apply the four features of a good paragraph: unity, coherency, completeness and consistency.
GoalParagraphs are the building blocks of every essay. Master these and your writing will flow.
slides.say: Welcome. Here are our objectives. By the end of this module, you will be able to explain what a paragraph is. You will name the three parts of a paragraph. And you will identify and apply the four features of a good paragraph: unity, coherency, completeness, and consistency. Paragraphs are the building blocks of every essay. Master these, and your writing will flow.
slides.example: A paragraph develops one main idea with several supporting sentences.

slides.html:
A paragraph is a group of related sentences that develop one main idea — called the topic sentence or controlling idea.

Think of it as a mini-essay about a single point. When the idea changes, you start a new paragraph.

KeyOne paragraph = one point, fully developed.
slides.say: A paragraph is a group of related sentences that develop one main idea, called the topic sentence, or controlling idea. Think of it as a mini essay about a single point. When the idea changes, you start a new paragraph. The key to remember: one paragraph equals one point, fully developed.
slides.example: A paragraph is a group of sentences that develop one single point.

slides.h: The three parts of a paragraph
slides.html:
A good paragraph has three parts:

  • Topic sentence — states the one controlling idea.
  • Supporting sentences — explain, give details and examples.
  • Conclusion — a closing sentence that rounds off the point.
slides.say: A good paragraph has three parts. The topic sentence, which states the one controlling idea. The supporting sentences, which explain, and give details and examples. And the conclusion, which is a closing sentence that rounds off the point.
slides.example: A good paragraph has a topic sentence, supporting sentences, and a concluding sentence.

slides.h: The four features of a good paragraph
slides.html:
A strong paragraph shows four features. We will study each in turn:

  • Unity — every sentence supports one idea.
  • Coherency — ideas flow smoothly, linked by transitions.
  • Completeness — the point is fully developed, with a solution.
  • Consistency — the writing never drifts from the topic sentence.
slides.say: A strong paragraph shows four features, and we will study each in turn. Unity, where every sentence supports one idea. Coherency, where the ideas flow smoothly, linked by transitions. Completeness, where the point is fully developed, including a solution. And consistency, where the writing never drifts away from the topic sentence.
slides.example: A strong paragraph shows unity, coherency, completeness, and consistency.

slides.html:
Unity means the whole paragraph throws light on a single idea. Every sentence agrees with, and supports, that point.

TestNo single sentence should wander off the point. If one does, it does not belong.
slides.say: Unity means the whole paragraph throws light on a single idea. Every sentence agrees with, and supports, that one point. Here is a test. No single sentence should wander off the point. If one does, it does not belong in the paragraph.
slides.example: Every sentence in a paragraph must support its one topic sentence.

slides.html:
Drug abuse has far-reaching effects. Physically, it causes heart disease, liver damage and a weak immune system. Mentally, it brings depression, anxiety and poor thinking. Socially, it destroys relationships, leading to isolation. Economically, users often lose their jobs. In all, drug abuse harms health, the mind, relationships and finances.

Every sentence supports one idea — the effects of drug abuse — so the paragraph is unified.
slides.say: Here is unity in action. Drug abuse has far reaching effects. Physically, it causes heart disease, liver damage, and a weak immune system. Mentally, it brings depression, anxiety, and poor thinking. Socially, it destroys relationships, leading to isolation. Economically, users often lose their jobs. In all, drug abuse harms health, the mind, relationships, and finances. Notice that every sentence supports one idea, the effects of drug abuse. So the paragraph is unified.
slides.example: Drug abuse has far-reaching effects. Physically, it damages the heart and liver. Mentally, it causes anxiety and confusion. Socially, it destroys families and friendships.

slides.html:
Coherency is the smooth flow of thought between sentences. Each sentence connects to the one before it.

We achieve it with transitions: for instance, however, in addition, as a result, finally. This keeps the explanation logical and orderly.
slides.say: Coherency is the smooth flow of thought between sentences. Each sentence connects to the one before it. We achieve coherency with transitions, such as: for instance, however, in addition, as a result, and finally. This keeps the explanation logical and orderly.
slides.example: Coherency is the smooth, logical flow of thought from one sentence to the next.

slides.html:
Bush fires are caused by natural and human factors. One natural cause is lightning, which ignites dry grass. However, human activity matters more. For instance, unattended campfires can spark flames. Additionally, sparks from machinery cause fires. The common thread is dry, combustible material, worsened by drought.

Notice the transition words guiding you from one cause to the next.
slides.say: Here is coherency in action. Bush fires are caused by natural and human factors. One natural cause is lightning, which ignites dry grass. However, human activity matters more. For instance, unattended campfires can spark flames. Additionally, sparks from machinery cause fires. The common thread is dry, combustible material, worsened by drought. Notice the transition words, guiding you from one cause to the next.
slides.example: Bush fires are caused by both natural and human factors. One natural cause is lightning during the dry season. A common human cause is the careless burning of farmland. Together, these spark fires that spread quickly.

slides.html:
Completeness means the point is fully developed. A paragraph is not a suspense show — it does not leave the reader hanging.

It creates a problem, explains it in full (with examples or figures), shows the effects, and offers a solution.
slides.say: Completeness means the point is fully developed. A paragraph is not a suspense show; it does not leave the reader hanging. It creates a problem, explains it in full, with examples or figures, shows the effects, and offers a solution.
slides.example: A complete paragraph develops its point fully, leaving no question unanswered.

slides.html:
One cause of poverty in Ghana is the lack of quality education. Many rural children cannot attend school due to high costs and poor facilities. This traps them in poverty, as the uneducated struggle to find good jobs. It also slows national growth, since an untrained workforce is less productive. Investing in education is therefore vital to break this cycle.

Main idea, full details, effects, and a solution — the point is complete.
slides.say: Here is completeness in action. One cause of poverty in Ghana is the lack of quality education. Many rural children cannot attend school due to high costs and poor facilities. This traps them in poverty, as the uneducated struggle to find good jobs. It also slows national growth, since an untrained workforce is less productive. Investing in education is therefore vital, to break this cycle. Notice the main idea, the full details, the effects, and a solution. The point is complete.
slides.example: One cause of poverty in Ghana is the lack of quality education. Many rural children leave school early to help on the farm. Without skills or certificates, they can only find low-paying work. As a result, the cycle of poverty continues into the next generation.

slides.html:
Consistency means the topic sentence and all its development agree from start to finish — the writing never deviates to another point.

In shortPick one point and stay on it until the paragraph ends.
slides.say: Consistency means the topic sentence and all its development agree, from start to finish. The writing never deviates to another point. In short: pick one point, and stay on it until the paragraph ends.
slides.example: Consistency means staying on one point, in one tense and one voice, throughout the paragraph.

slides.html:
The main cause of illegal mining in Ghana is unemployment and poverty. Many rural people turn to "galamsey" to survive, lacking formal jobs. The promise of quick money from gold attracts those struggling to make ends meet. This hardship, with weak enforcement, lets illegal mining flourish. To stop it, we must create jobs and strengthen the laws.

From cause to behaviour to solution — every sentence stays on the one cause.
slides.say: Here is consistency in action. The main cause of illegal mining in Ghana is unemployment and poverty. Many rural people turn to galamsey to survive, lacking formal jobs. The promise of quick money from gold attracts those struggling to make ends meet. This hardship, with weak enforcement, lets illegal mining flourish. To stop it, we must create jobs and strengthen the laws. From cause, to behaviour, to solution, every sentence stays on the one cause.
slides.example: The main cause of illegal mining in Ghana is unemployment. Many rural youth cannot find decent jobs after school. To survive, they turn to galamsey for quick money. This desperation keeps the destructive practice alive.

slides.html:
Let us recap:

  • A paragraph develops one main idea (the topic sentence).
  • Its parts: topic sentence, supporting sentences, conclusion.
  • The four features: Unity (one idea), Coherency (smooth flow), Completeness (fully developed + solution), Consistency (never drifts).
Well doneStrong paragraphs make strong essays. Practise the four features on the steps above!
slides.say: Let us recap. A paragraph develops one main idea, the topic sentence. Its parts are the topic sentence, the supporting sentences, and the conclusion. And the four features are: Unity, meaning one idea. Coherency, meaning smooth flow. Completeness, meaning fully developed with a solution. And consistency, meaning the writing never drifts. Well done. Strong paragraphs make strong essays.
slides.example: A good paragraph shows unity, coherency, completeness, and consistency around one clear point.

rewrite.weak: Drug abuse harms the body, causing heart and liver disease. It also causes depression and anxiety. My uncle drives a taxi in Kumasi. Socially, it destroys relationships with family and friends.
rewrite.hint: This paragraph breaks unity. Find and remove the sentence that does not belong, and keep it focused on one idea.
rewrite.model: Drug abuse harms the body, causing heart and liver disease. It also causes mental problems such as depression and anxiety. Socially, it destroys relationships with family and friends, leading to isolation.
rubric: The off-topic sentence (about the taxi) is removed
Every remaining sentence supports the one idea (unity)

points.passage: Bush fires are caused by natural and human factors. One natural cause is lightning, which ignites dry grass during storms. However, human activity contributes more: unattended campfires and discarded cigarettes can spark flames. Slash-and-burn farming sometimes spreads beyond its boundaries, and sparks from machinery cause fires too. The common thread is dry, combustible material, worsened by drought.
model: Main idea: bush fires have natural and human causes
Natural cause: lightning on dry grass
Human causes: campfires, cigarettes, slash-and-burn, machinery sparks
Common factor: dry material made worse by drought

points: Topic: the effects of drug abuse
frompoints.instruction: Write ONE unified paragraph on the effects of drug abuse. Every sentence must support that one idea.
frompoints.model: Drug abuse has serious effects. Physically, it leads to heart disease and liver damage. Mentally, it brings on depression and anxiety. Socially, it breaks relationships and causes isolation. Economically, many users lose their jobs. In all, drug abuse damages the body, the mind, relationships and one's finances.
rubric: A clear topic sentence opens the paragraph
Every sentence supports the one idea (unity)
A closing sentence rounds it off

question.q: Write a complete paragraph on ONE cause of poverty in Ghana. Make sure it shows unity, coherency, completeness and consistency.
question.model: One major cause of poverty in Ghana is the lack of quality education. Many children in rural areas cannot attend school because of high costs and poor facilities. As a result, they grow up without the skills to secure good jobs, and so the cycle of poverty continues. Furthermore, an untrained workforce slows the whole nation's growth. Investing in education is therefore essential to break this cycle.
rubric: Every sentence stays on that one cause (unity & consistency)
The point is fully developed and ends with a solution (completeness)

title: Paragraph Transitions
blurb: The linking words and sentence patterns that make ideas flow and join paragraphs into one essay.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Explain what transitions do and why they matter.
  • Choose the right transition for order, addition, contrast, time, result and more.
  • Use cohesive devices and compound/complex sentences to join ideas smoothly.
GoalTransitions are the chains that join sentences and paragraphs into one flowing essay.
slides.say: Welcome. Here are our objectives. By the end of this module, you will be able to explain what transitions do, and why they matter. You will choose the right transition for order, addition, contrast, time, result, and more. And you will use cohesive devices, and compound and complex sentences, to join ideas smoothly. Transitions are the chains that join sentences and paragraphs into one flowing essay.
slides.example: Transitions are words and phrases that join sentences and paragraphs into one smooth flow.

slides.html:
Transitional words and phrases show the order, time, cause or other relation between ideas in a paragraph.

They tell the reader the direction of the writing is changing, and they can sit at the beginning, middle or end of a paragraph.

Why they matterThey create coherency — and join your paragraphs into one whole essay.
slides.say: Transitional words and phrases show the order, the time, the cause, or some other relation between ideas in a paragraph. They tell the reader that the direction of the writing is changing. And they can sit at the beginning, the middle, or the end of a paragraph. Why do they matter? They create coherency, and they join your paragraphs together into one whole essay.
slides.example: Transitions such as 'first', 'in addition', and 'as a result' guide the reader from one idea to the next.

slides.html:
To introduce your first point or set an order:

  • First of all
  • Firstly / In the first place
  • To begin with
To begin with, the first cause of flooding is poor drainage.
slides.say: To introduce your first point, or to set an order, use transitions like these. First of all. Firstly, or in the first place. To begin with. For example: To begin with, the first cause of flooding is poor drainage.
slides.example: To begin with, the first cause of flooding is poor drainage.

slides.html:
To add more points to the same idea:

  • Also, in addition, besides
  • Moreover, furthermore
  • Equally important, not only that but…
In addition, blocked gutters make the flooding worse.
slides.say: To add more points to the same idea, use: Also, in addition, besides. Moreover, furthermore. Equally important. Or, not only that, but. For example: In addition, blocked gutters make the flooding worse.
slides.example: In addition, blocked gutters make the flooding worse.

slides.html:
For your final point:

  • Finally, lastly, last

To conclude the whole essay:

  • To conclude, in conclusion
  • To crown it all, in a nutshell
slides.say: For your final point, use: Finally, lastly, or last. And to conclude the whole essay, use: To conclude, in conclusion, to crown it all, or in a nutshell.
slides.example: Finally, the dumping of refuse into drains completes the problem. In conclusion, flooding in our town is largely caused by human carelessness.

slides.html:
Comparison (showing things are alike):

  • In the same way, likewise, similarly

Contrast (showing differences):

  • However, on the other hand, in contrast
  • Although, nevertheless, on the contrary, instead
slides.say: For comparison, when showing that things are alike, use: In the same way, likewise, or similarly. For contrast, when showing differences, use: However, on the other hand, in contrast. Also: although, nevertheless, on the contrary, or instead.
slides.example: Town life is fast and crowded; in contrast, village life is slow and peaceful.

slides.html:
Example: for example, for instance, to illustrate, namely.

Emphasis: certainly, indeed, in fact, of course.

Concession (granting a point): granted, naturally, of course.

For instance, last year three classrooms were flooded.
slides.say: To give an example, use: for example, for instance, to illustrate, or namely. For emphasis, use: certainly, indeed, in fact, or of course. And for concession, when you grant a point, use: granted, naturally, or of course. For instance: last year, three classrooms were flooded.
slides.example: For instance, last year three classrooms were flooded after a single night of rain.

slides.html:
To show time order in a story or process:

  • First → next → then → soon → afterward → finally
  • Before, after, while, until
  • Immediately, eventually, no sooner than…
Soon afterward, the rains stopped and the water drained away.
slides.say: To show time order, in a story or a process, use words like: first, next, then, soon, afterward, and finally. Also: before, after, while, and until. And: immediately, eventually, or no sooner than. For example: Soon afterward, the rains stopped, and the water drained away.
slides.example: Soon afterward, the rains stopped and the water slowly drained away.

slides.html:
To show a result or effect:

  • As a result
  • Consequently
  • The result is that…
  • Therefore, so
The gutters were blocked; as a result, the road flooded.
slides.say: To show a result, or an effect, use: As a result. Consequently. The result is that. Therefore, or so. For example: The gutters were blocked; as a result, the road flooded.
slides.example: The gutters were blocked; as a result, the road flooded.

slides.html:
Beyond transitions, these cohesive devices link sentences smoothly:

  • Pronouns — he, she, they, it, this, that (replace a noun)
  • Conjunctions — and, but, or, so, because, although
  • Repetition — repeating a key word or a synonym
  • Logical connectors — firstly, moreover, however, consequently
slides.say: Beyond transitions, these cohesive devices link sentences smoothly. Pronouns, such as he, she, they, it, this, and that, which replace a noun. Conjunctions, such as and, but, or, so, because, and although. Repetition, where you repeat a key word, or a synonym, to create continuity. And logical connectors, such as firstly, moreover, however, and consequently.
slides.example: Kofi lost his book, but he found it under the desk where he had left it.

slides.html:
A compound sentence joins two complete ideas with a conjunction — linking related ideas in one sentence:

  • so — He was tired, so he went to bed early.
  • or — We can go to the park, or we can stay home.
  • yet — She studied hard, yet she didn't pass.
  • nor — She doesn't like apples, nor oranges.
slides.say: A compound sentence joins two complete ideas with a conjunction, linking related ideas in one sentence. For example, with so: He was tired, so he went to bed early. With or: We can go to the park, or we can stay home. With yet: She studied hard, yet she did not pass. And with nor: She doesn't like apples, nor oranges.
slides.example: The gutters were blocked, so the rainwater spilled onto the road.

slides.html:
A complex sentence adds a dependent clause for depth. Useful linking clauses:

  • Time: when, before, after, since, while, until — Call me when you arrive.
  • Condition: if, unless — You won't pass unless you work hard.
  • Purpose: so that, in order to — She saved so that she could buy a car.
  • Reason: because, since, as — He left early because he was tired.
slides.say: A complex sentence adds a dependent clause, for depth. Here are useful linking clauses. For time: when, before, after, since, while, and until. For example: Call me when you arrive. For condition: if, and unless. For example: You will not pass, unless you work hard. For purpose: so that, and in order to. For example: She saved, so that she could buy a car. And for reason: because, since, and as. For example: He left early, because he was tired.
slides.example: Although the gutters were cleared, the road still flooded because the rain was heavy.

slides.html:
Flooding in our town has several causes. To begin with, the gutters are blocked with rubbish. In addition, houses are built on waterways. However, the biggest cause is poor planning. As a result, even light rain floods the streets. In a nutshell, blocked drains, illegal building and poor planning all cause our floods.

See how the transitions guide you from point to point.
slides.say: Here are transitions in action. Flooding in our town has several causes. To begin with, the gutters are blocked with rubbish. In addition, houses are built on waterways. However, the biggest cause is poor planning. As a result, even light rain floods the streets. In a nutshell, blocked drains, illegal building, and poor planning all cause our floods. See how the transitions guide you, from point to point.
slides.example: Flooding in our town has several causes. To begin with, the gutters are blocked with refuse. In addition, houses are built on waterways. As a result, even a short rain floods the streets. Finally, we must clear our drains to solve this problem.

slides.html:
Let us recap:

  • Transitions show the relation between ideas and create coherency.
  • Match the transition to the job: order, addition, contrast, time, result, conclusion.
  • Cohesive devices (pronouns, conjunctions, repetition) bind sentences.
  • Compound and complex sentences link related ideas smoothly.
Well doneWith transitions, your separate sentences and paragraphs become one flowing essay.
slides.say: Let us recap. Transitions show the relation between ideas, and they create coherency. Match the transition to its job: order, addition, contrast, time, result, or conclusion. Cohesive devices, such as pronouns, conjunctions, and repetition, bind your sentences together. And compound and complex sentences link related ideas smoothly. Well done. With transitions, your separate sentences and paragraphs become one flowing essay.
slides.example: With transitions, separate sentences and paragraphs join into one flowing piece of writing.

rewrite.weak: The road flooded. The gutters were blocked. Houses are built on the waterway. The council did nothing. People lost their property.
rewrite.hint: Join these choppy sentences with transitions (to begin with, in addition, as a result, in a nutshell) so they flow.
rewrite.model: Our road floods for several reasons. To begin with, the gutters are blocked with rubbish. In addition, houses are built on the waterway. Because the council did nothing, the problem grew worse. As a result, many people lost their property.
rubric: At least three different transitions are used
The choppy sentences now flow smoothly
A compound or complex sentence is used

points.passage: Flooding in our town has several causes. To begin with, the gutters are blocked with rubbish. In addition, houses are built on waterways. However, the biggest cause is poor town planning. As a result, even light rain floods the streets.
model: Main idea: flooding has several causes
Cause 1: gutters blocked with rubbish
Cause 2: houses built on waterways
Main cause: poor town planning
Result: even light rain floods the streets

frompoints.instruction: Write ONE paragraph from these points, using transitions for order, addition and a conclusion.
frompoints.model: Reading is a valuable habit for many reasons. To begin with, it builds a rich vocabulary. In addition, it strengthens the imagination, allowing us to picture new worlds. Finally, it sharpens our focus and patience. In a nutshell, reading grows the mind in several powerful ways.
rubric: An ordering transition opens the points (to begin with/firstly)
An addition transition is used (in addition/moreover)
A concluding transition closes it (in a nutshell/to conclude)

question.q: Write a short paragraph on the causes of road accidents, using at least FOUR different transitions (order, addition, contrast, result).
question.model: Road accidents have several causes. To begin with, many drivers are over-speeding. In addition, some vehicles are poorly maintained. However, the worst cause is careless overtaking. As a result, lives and property are lost every day. In a nutshell, speed, bad vehicles and careless overtaking all cause road accidents.
rubric: At least four different transitions are used
Every sentence supports the one topic (unity)
The tone and flow read smoothly

title: Parts of a Paragraph
blurb: Build a paragraph step by step: topic sentence, supporting sentences, examples, results, effects, solution and conclusion.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Identify the main idea and supporting details in a paragraph.
  • Name the parts of a paragraph: topic sentence, body, conclusion.
  • Build a full paragraph: topic sentence → major support → minor support → example → result → effect → solution → conclusion.
slides.say: Welcome. Here are our objectives. By the end of this module, you will be able to identify the main idea and the supporting details in a paragraph. You will name the parts of a paragraph: the topic sentence, the body, and the conclusion. And you will build a full paragraph, step by step, from the topic sentence, through major and minor support, an example, the result, the effect, a solution, and a conclusion.
slides.example: A full paragraph moves from a topic sentence through supporting details to a conclusion.

slides.html:
Every paragraph has two layers:

  • Main idea — the single point the paragraph is about.
  • Supporting details — the sentences that explain, prove or expand the main idea.
To find themAsk: what is this paragraph mostly about? (main idea). What does it say to back that up? (details).
slides.say: Every paragraph has two layers. The main idea, which is the single point the paragraph is about. And the supporting details, which are the sentences that explain, prove, or expand the main idea. To find them, ask yourself two questions. What is this paragraph mostly about? That gives the main idea. And, what does it say to back that up? Those are the details.
slides.example: Bees are essential to our environment because they pollinate the plants that feed us.

slides.html:
Bees are essential to our environment because they pollinate plants. Pollination lets plants make fruits and seeds. Without bees, many plants could not reproduce, so we would have fewer fruits and vegetables. Bees also keep many kinds of plants alive. Protecting bees, therefore, protects our environment.

Main idea: bees are essential because they pollinate. Details: pollination → fruits/seeds; no bees → no reproduction; fewer foods; biodiversity; so protect bees.
slides.say: Let us find the main idea. Bees are essential to our environment because they pollinate plants. Pollination lets plants make fruits and seeds. Without bees, many plants could not reproduce, so we would have fewer fruits and vegetables. Bees also keep many kinds of plants alive. Protecting bees, therefore, protects our environment. The main idea is: bees are essential because they pollinate. The details are: pollination makes fruits and seeds; without bees plants cannot reproduce; we would have fewer foods; bees protect biodiversity; so we must protect bees.
slides.example: Bees are essential to our environment because they pollinate plants. As they move from flower to flower, they help crops produce fruit and seeds. Without bees, much of our food supply would fail.

slides.html:
A paragraph is a mini-essay, so it has three parts:

  • Introduction — the topic sentence
  • Body — major and minor supporting sentences
  • Conclusion — a closing remark
slides.say: A paragraph is a mini essay, so it has three parts. The introduction, which is the topic sentence. The body, which is made of major and minor supporting sentences. And the conclusion, which is a closing remark.
slides.example: A paragraph is a mini-essay: it has an introduction, a body, and a conclusion.

slides.html:
The topic sentence is the first sentence. It states the point the paragraph will explain — the controlling idea — and it summarises the whole paragraph.

RuleIt must be a complete sentence with a full stop — never just a phrase.
The first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care.
slides.say: The topic sentence is the first sentence. It states the point the paragraph will explain, the controlling idea, and it summarises the whole paragraph. Here is the rule. It must be a complete sentence, with a full stop, never just a phrase. For example: The first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care.
slides.example: The first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care.

slides.html:
The major supporting sentence is the second sentence. It restates the topic sentence in simpler words, using synonyms.

  • inadequate parental care → parents do not take enough care
  • poverty → they do not have money

Start it with a noun or pronoun (usually the subject). Be specific (Some, not Many). Do not begin with since, because, when.
slides.say: The major supporting sentence is the second sentence. It restates the topic sentence in simpler words, using synonyms. For example, inadequate parental care becomes, parents do not take enough care. And poverty becomes, they do not have money. Start it with a noun or pronoun, usually the subject. Be specific: say some, not many. And do not begin it with since, because, or when.
slides.example: Many parents simply do not take good care of their children.

slides.html:
Minor supporting sentences add more detail. Each sentence explains the one before it.

To find them, keep asking: How? What happened? And then? Why?

Some parents do not take good care of their children. They fail to provide food, shelter and clothing. They also give no moral training, because they are too busy working.
slides.say: Minor supporting sentences add more detail. Each sentence explains the one before it. To find them, keep asking: How? What happened? And then? Why? For example: Some parents do not take good care of their children. They fail to provide food, shelter, and clothing. They also give no moral training, because they are too busy working.
slides.example: They fail to provide food, shelter and clothing, and they give little moral guidance.

slides.html:
Prove your claim is real with an example or instance. Introduce it with for instance, for example, to illustrate, and use facts and figures where you can.

For instance, many children in the cities sleep on the streets, such as at Kejetia and Nkrumah Circle.
slides.say: Prove your claim is real with an example, or instance. Introduce it with, for instance, for example, or to illustrate, and use facts and figures where you can. For example: For instance, many children in the cities sleep on the streets, such as at Kejetia, and Nkrumah Circle.
slides.example: For instance, many children in the cities sleep on the streets at places such as Kejetia.

slides.html:
Now link your explanation back to the topic. Ask: and so, what?

The result is that these children go out in search of their needs, exposing them to risky relationships.
WhyDetails alone do not prove the point — the result connects them to the topic.
slides.say: Now link your explanation back to the topic. Ask: and so, what? For example: The result is that these children go out in search of their needs, exposing them to risky relationships. Why does this matter? Because details alone do not prove the point. The result is what connects them back to the topic.
slides.example: The result is that these children roam about in search of their basic needs.

slides.html:
State how the result causes the topic. Use in effect, consequently, and show the effect.

The effect is that the girls engage in premarital sex, leading to teenage pregnancy. Some may even die through unsafe abortion.
slides.say: State how the result causes the topic. Use, in effect, or consequently, and show the effect. For example: The effect is that the girls engage in premarital sex, leading to teenage pregnancy. Some may even die through unsafe abortion.
slides.example: The effect is that some girls engage in premarital sex, which leads to teenage pregnancy.

slides.html:
Where the topic is a problem, offer a solution — a positive statement of the claim. (Not every essay needs one.)

Parents are therefore advised to offer adequate care to their children, as this helps prevent teenage pregnancy.
slides.say: Where the topic is a problem, offer a solution, which is a positive statement of the claim. Note that not every essay needs one. For example: Parents are therefore advised to offer adequate care to their children, as this helps prevent teenage pregnancy.
slides.example: Parents are therefore advised to give their children adequate care and attention.

slides.html:
End with a definite remark that makes your point stand — re-emphasise the topic sentence, or restate your stance/solution.

EffectIt signals the end of the paragraph and reinforces the main point.
slides.say: End with a definite remark that makes your point stand. You can re-emphasise the topic sentence, or restate your stance, or your solution. This signals the end of the paragraph, and reinforces the main point.
slides.example: Clearly, inadequate parental care remains a major cause of teenage pregnancy in our communities.

slides.html: The first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. [topic] Some parents do not take good care of their children. [major] They fail to provide food, shelter and clothing, and give no moral training, because they are too busy working. [minor] For instance, many children sleep on the streets at Kejetia. [example] The result is that they seek their needs elsewhere. [result] The effect is that many girls fall pregnant. [effect] Parents are therefore advised to care properly for their children. [solution/conclusion]
slides.say: Here is a model paragraph with all the parts together. The first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. That is the topic sentence. Some parents do not take good care of their children. That is the major support. They fail to provide food, shelter and clothing, and give no moral training, because they are too busy working. Those are the minor supporting details. For instance, many children sleep on the streets at Kejetia. That is the example. The result is that they seek their needs elsewhere. That is the result. The effect is that many girls fall pregnant. That is the effect. Parents are therefore advised to care properly for their children. That is the solution, and the conclusion.
slides.example: The first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. Many parents do not take good care of their children. They fail to provide food, shelter and clothing, and offer little moral guidance, because they are busy working. For instance, some children sleep on the streets at Kejetia. The result is that they roam in search of their needs, and the effect is that some girls fall pregnant through premarital sex. Parents are therefore advised to care properly for their children.

slides.html:
Let us recap a paragraph, in order:

  • Topic sentence — a complete sentence stating the point.
  • Major support — restate it in simpler words.
  • Minor support — details answering how/what/why.
  • Example → result → effect → solution.
  • Conclusion — a definite closing remark.
Well doneYou can now build a full, convincing paragraph from a single point.
slides.say: Let us recap a paragraph, in order. The topic sentence, a complete sentence stating the point. The major support, which restates it in simpler words. The minor support, with details answering how, what, and why. Then an example, the result, the effect, and a solution. And finally, a conclusion, a definite closing remark. Well done. You can now build a full, convincing paragraph from a single point.
slides.example: A complete paragraph runs from topic sentence to supporting details, example, result, effect, solution, and conclusion.

rewrite.weak: Inadequate parental care. Because parents are busy. Many children suffer a lot in this country.
rewrite.hint: Turn this into the opening of a proper paragraph: a complete topic sentence, then a major supporting sentence. Don't begin with 'because'; be specific (not 'many').
rewrite.model: The first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. Some parents do not take good care of their children, as they are too busy working.
rubric: The topic sentence is a complete sentence with a full stop
The major supporting sentence restates the point in simpler words

points.passage: Trees are vital to our planet because they produce oxygen. Through photosynthesis, trees absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen into the air. This cleans the air and gives us the oxygen we need to breathe. Trees also offer shade and shelter for many animals, and they prevent soil erosion by holding the soil with their roots.
model: Main idea: trees are vital because they produce oxygen
They absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen (photosynthesis)
They clean the air / give us oxygen to breathe
They provide shade and shelter for animals
They prevent soil erosion

points: Topic sentence: Reading books opens a world of knowledge.
major: it takes us to new places and times
minor: it helps us learn new words and ideas
example/effect: it teaches lessons and sparks creativity
frompoints.instruction: Build ONE paragraph from these parts: topic sentence, then major support, then minor support, then an example and a closing remark.
frompoints.model: Reading books opens a world of knowledge. It carries us to new places and times we could never visit in real life. As we read, we also pick up new words and fresh ideas. For instance, a single story can teach a valuable lesson and spark our creativity. In all, reading is a powerful way to grow the mind.
rubric: Begins with the topic sentence (complete sentence)
A major supporting sentence restates the point

question.q: Write a COMPLETE paragraph on one benefit of exercise. Include a topic sentence, major support, minor support, an example, and a conclusion.
question.model: Exercise keeps the body strong and healthy. Regular activity builds our muscles and bones and protects the heart. It does this by keeping the blood flowing and burning excess fat. For instance, a person who walks briskly for thirty minutes a day lowers the risk of heart disease and diabetes. As a result, an active person stays fit and energetic. Everyone should therefore make time to exercise each day.
rubric: Opens with a clear topic sentence
Has a major supporting sentence (restates the point)
Includes a concrete example or figure
Ends with a definite concluding remark

title: Types of Paragraphs
blurb: Descriptive, narrative, expository and persuasive paragraphs — with sensory detail and figurative language.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Identify the four types of paragraph and each one's purpose.
  • Tell the mode, purpose and audience of a text.
  • Write a vivid descriptive paragraph using the five senses and figurative language.
slides.say: Welcome. Here are our objectives. By the end of this module, you will be able to identify the four types of paragraph, and the purpose of each. You will tell the mode, the purpose, and the audience of a text. And you will write a vivid descriptive paragraph, using the five senses, and figurative language.
slides.example: Descriptive, narrative, expository and persuasive paragraphs each serve a different purpose.

slides.html:
Different paragraphs serve different purposes:

  • Descriptive — paints a picture of a person, place or thing.
  • Narrative — tells a story in time order.
  • Expository — explains or informs.
  • Persuasive (argumentative) — takes a side and convinces.
slides.say: Different paragraphs serve different purposes. A descriptive paragraph paints a picture of a person, place, or thing. A narrative paragraph tells a story, in time order. An expository paragraph explains, or informs. And a persuasive, or argumentative, paragraph takes a side, and convinces the reader.
slides.example: There are four main types of paragraph: descriptive, narrative, expository and persuasive.

slides.html:
Read a text and ask three questions: what is its mode (type)? its purpose? its audience?

  • Children laughing on swings, golden sun → Descriptive (describe a scene)
  • Maya finds and cares for a kitten → Narrative (tell a story)
  • "Recycling should be in every school…" → Persuasive (convince)
  • "A rainbow forms when sunlight…" → Expository (explain)
slides.say: Read a text and ask three questions. What is its mode, or type? What is its purpose? And who is its audience? For example: children laughing on swings, with a golden sun, is descriptive, because it describes a scene. Maya finds and cares for a kitten is narrative, because it tells a story. Recycling should be in every school, is persuasive, because it convinces. And, a rainbow forms when sunlight passes through rain, is expository, because it explains.
slides.example: Before writing, decide the mode, the purpose, and the audience of your paragraph.

slides.html:
A descriptive paragraph paints a vivid picture using sensory details — sight, sound, smell, taste and touch.

It leans on strong adjectives and adverbs to help the reader see the scene.

AimMake the reader feel they are right there.
slides.say: A descriptive paragraph paints a vivid picture, using sensory details: sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch. It leans on strong adjectives and adverbs to help the reader see the scene. The aim is to make the reader feel they are right there.
slides.example: A descriptive paragraph paints a vivid picture so the reader can see, hear and feel the scene.

slides.html:
A descriptive paragraph still uses the three building blocks:

  • Topic sentence: Soccer is an exciting sport enjoyed by millions.
  • Major supporting sentence: The game is physically demanding and needs teamwork.
  • Supporting details: Players run, pass and communicate; a midfielder controls the ball and finds open teammates.
slides.say: A descriptive paragraph still uses the three building blocks. The topic sentence, for example: Soccer is an exciting sport enjoyed by millions. The major supporting sentence: The game is physically demanding, and needs teamwork. And the supporting details: Players run, pass, and communicate; a midfielder controls the ball, and finds open teammates.
slides.example: The old market buzzed with life: traders called out their prices while the smoky scent of grilled plantain filled the warm afternoon air.

slides.html:
Sight (visual imagery) — colour, shape, size, appearance.

A vibrant expanse of emerald grass, crisp white lines, goalposts gleaming white.

Sound (auditory imagery) — use describing words and onomatopoeia.

The engine roared to life, then settled into a steady purr; the tyres crunched over gravel.
slides.say: Sight, or visual imagery, describes colour, shape, size, and appearance. For example: a vibrant expanse of emerald grass, crisp white lines, and goalposts gleaming white. Sound, or auditory imagery, uses describing words and onomatopoeia. For example: the engine roared to life, then settled into a steady purr, and the tyres crunched over the gravel.
slides.example: A vibrant expanse of emerald grass stretched out, crisp white lines gleaming as the crowd roared in the distance.

slides.html:
Smell (olfactory): the pungent aroma of fresh fish mingled with the earthy scent of ripe tomatoes at Makola Market.

Taste (gustatory): the buttery, flaky crust crumbled on the tongue; a tangy burst of apple.

Touch (tactile): the smooth, cool surface of the wooden table; edges rounded and polished.
slides.say: Smell, the olfactory sense: for example, the pungent aroma of fresh fish, mingled with the earthy scent of ripe tomatoes, at Makola Market. Taste, the gustatory sense: for example, the buttery, flaky crust crumbled on the tongue; a tangy burst of apple. And touch, the tactile sense: for example, the smooth, cool surface of the wooden table; its edges rounded and polished.
slides.example: The pungent aroma of fresh fish mingled with the earthy smell of rain, while the cool, damp breeze brushed against our skin.

slides.html:
Go beyond the literal with figures of speech:

  • Metaphor — one thing is another: the table was a silent witness to years of study.
  • Simile — compares with like/as: rain fell like a gentle curtain.
  • Personification — human qualities to objects: the rain breathes life into the earth.
slides.say: Go beyond the literal with figures of speech. A metaphor says one thing is another: the table was a silent witness to years of study. A simile compares using like or as: rain fell like a gentle curtain. And personification gives human qualities to objects: the rain breathes life into the earth.
slides.example: The waves whispered to the shore, and the golden sun smiled down on the busy beach.

slides.html:
Arrange your details in a clear order:

  • Spatial — by position: left to right, top to bottom, near to far.
  • Chronological — by time or steps: first, next, finally.
  • Order of importance — the most striking detail first.
TipUse transitions (to the left, next, in the distance) to guide the reader.
slides.say: Arrange your details in a clear order. Spatial order goes by position: left to right, top to bottom, or near to far. Chronological order goes by time, or by steps: first, next, finally. And order of importance puts the most striking detail first. A tip: use transitions, such as, to the left, next, and in the distance, to guide the reader.
slides.example: To the left stood the tall library; next to it, a shady mango tree; and in the distance, the blue hills rose softly.

slides.html:
End a descriptive paragraph by summarising the picture or giving a final thought that ties back to the topic sentence.

In summary, the cosy library — with its mahogany shelves and soft armchairs — is a perfect retreat for any reader.
slides.say: End a descriptive paragraph by summarising the picture, or giving a final thought that ties back to the topic sentence. For example: In summary, the cosy library, with its mahogany shelves and soft armchairs, is a perfect retreat for any reader.
slides.example: In short, the cosy library, with its mahogany shelves and soft armchairs, felt like a peaceful second home.

slides.html:
A narrative paragraph tells a story, in time order. It has characters, events and often a small lesson.

One afternoon, Maya found a shivering kitten under a bush. She brought it inside, gave it milk, and named it Whiskers. They soon became best friends.

Use time transitions: one day, then, soon, finally.
slides.say: A narrative paragraph tells a story, in time order. It has characters, events, and often a small lesson. For example: One afternoon, Maya found a shivering kitten under a bush. She brought it inside, gave it milk, and named it Whiskers. They soon became best friends. Use time transitions, such as: one day, then, soon, and finally.
slides.example: One afternoon, Maya found a shivering kitten under a bush. She carried it home, fed it warm milk, and named it Lucky. From that day, the two became inseparable friends.

slides.html:
An expository paragraph explains or informs — clearly and factually, with no opinion.

A rainbow forms when sunlight passes through rain droplets. The light bends and splits into seven colours, forming an arc across the sky.

Aim for clarity and correct facts, often in logical or step order.
slides.say: An expository paragraph explains, or informs, clearly and factually, with no opinion. For example: A rainbow forms when sunlight passes through rain droplets. The light bends, and splits into seven colours, forming an arc across the sky. Aim for clarity, and correct facts, often in a logical, or step, order.
slides.example: A rainbow forms when sunlight passes through rain droplets. The light bends, splitting into its colours. Each droplet acts like a tiny prism, and together they form the familiar arc across the sky.

slides.html:
A persuasive (argumentative) paragraph takes a side and convinces the reader with reasons, and often answers the opposite view.

Recycling should be part of every school's routine. It cuts waste, saves resources and teaches care for the Earth. Some say it is too much work, but the benefits are well worth it.
slides.say: A persuasive, or argumentative, paragraph takes a side, and convinces the reader with reasons, and often answers the opposite view. For example: Recycling should be part of every school's routine. It cuts waste, saves resources, and teaches care for the Earth. Some say it is too much work, but the benefits are well worth it.
slides.example: Recycling should be part of every school's routine. It cuts waste, saves resources, and teaches pupils responsibility. If every class recycled, our town would be far cleaner. Let us all begin today.

slides.html:
Let us recap:

  • Four types: descriptive (paint a picture), narrative (tell a story), expository (explain), persuasive (convince).
  • Read for mode, purpose and audience.
  • Descriptive writing uses the five senses, figurative language, a clear order, and a strong concluding sentence.
Well doneNow you can pick the right kind of paragraph for any writing task.
slides.say: Let us recap. There are four types of paragraph: descriptive, which paints a picture; narrative, which tells a story; expository, which explains; and persuasive, which convinces. Always read for the mode, the purpose, and the audience. And remember, descriptive writing uses the five senses, figurative language, a clear order, and a strong concluding sentence. Well done. Now you can pick the right kind of paragraph for any writing task.
slides.example: Choose the right type of paragraph, descriptive, narrative, expository or persuasive, for each writing task.

rewrite.weak: The market was busy. There were many people and things to buy. It smelled of food.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite this as a vivid DESCRIPTIVE paragraph using at least three senses (sight, sound, smell) and one figure of speech.
rewrite.model: Makola Market buzzed like a giant hive at noon. Traders called out their prices while shoppers squeezed past pyramids of red tomatoes and golden plantains. The warm air carried the smoky scent of grilled kebabs mixed with the sharp tang of dried fish. Everywhere, colour and noise spilled together in a lively, unforgettable scene.
rubric: At least three senses are used (sight, sound, smell)
Strong adjectives create a vivid picture
At least one simile/metaphor/personification is used
A clear concluding impression ends it

points.passage: A rainbow happens when sunlight passes through rain droplets in the sky. The light bends, then splits into different colours, creating a beautiful arc. The colours are red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo and violet. You usually see rainbows after it rains, when the sun shines again.
model: Main idea: how a rainbow is formed
Mode of writing: Expository (it explains)
Purpose: to explain the process of how rainbows form
Audience: learners / general public

points: Sound: rain drumming on the roof
frompoints.instruction: Write ONE descriptive paragraph using these sensory points, organised from what you see to what you feel.
frompoints.model: The rainy evening wrapped the town in a soft grey light. Shining puddles spread across the road, rippling with every fresh drop. On the roof, the rain drummed a steady, soothing beat. The air carried the rich smell of wet earth, cool and fresh against my skin. It was the kind of evening that made the whole world feel calm and new.
rubric: Opens with a clear topic sentence
Uses sight, sound, smell and touch

question.q: Write a DESCRIPTIVE paragraph (5–7 sentences) about a place you love. Use at least three senses, a clear order, a topic sentence and a concluding sentence.
question.model: My grandmother's kitchen is the warmest place I know. As you step in, the smell of freshly baked bread mixes with herbs from the garden outside. A wide wooden table sits in the centre, draped with a checkered cloth and a bowl of red apples. Copper pots gleam on their hooks, catching the afternoon light, while old family photos line the walls. Every corner glows with warmth and love. In all, the kitchen feels like the very heart of our home.
rubric: Begins with a clear topic sentence
Includes a figure of speech or vivid image
Ends with a strong concluding sentence

title: Narrative Paragraphs
blurb: Tell a story well: chronological order, characters, setting, plot — all in the past tense.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Explain what a narrative paragraph is.
  • Use its four parts: topic sentence, major support, minor support, concluding sentence.
  • Build a story with chronological order, characters, setting and plot — in the past tense.
slides.say: Welcome. Here are our objectives. By the end of this module, you will be able to explain what a narrative paragraph is. You will use its four parts: the topic sentence, the major support, the minor support, and the concluding sentence. And you will build a story with chronological order, characters, setting, and plot, all written in the past tense.
slides.example: A narrative paragraph tells a story in time order, with characters, a setting and a plot.

slides.html:
A narrative paragraph tells a story or recounts an event. It follows chronological order and usually has characters, a setting and a plot.

Key ruleNarratives are written in the past tense — the events have already happened.
slides.say: A narrative paragraph tells a story, or recounts an event. It follows chronological order, and usually has characters, a setting, and a plot. Here is the key rule. Narratives are written in the past tense, because the events have already happened.
slides.example: A narrative paragraph tells a story or recounts an event, written in the past tense.

slides.html:
A narrative paragraph has four parts:

  • Topic sentence — introduces the story. Last summer, I had an unforgettable adventure at the Kwahu ridge.
  • Major supporting sentence — a key event. We hiked down the mountains, which offered breathtaking views.
  • Minor supporting sentences — the details of the story.
  • Concluding sentence — wraps up or reflects.
slides.say: A narrative paragraph has four parts. The topic sentence, which introduces the story. For example: Last summer, I had an unforgettable adventure at the Kwahu ridge. The major supporting sentence, which gives a key event. For example: We hiked down the mountains, which offered breathtaking views. The minor supporting sentences, which give the details of the story. And the concluding sentence, which wraps up, or reflects on, the event.
slides.example: A narrative has a topic sentence, major and minor supporting details, and a concluding sentence.

slides.html: Last summer, I had an unforgettable adventure at the Kwahu ridge. [topic] We hiked down the mountains, which offered breathtaking views and tough terrain. [major] The trail was steep and narrow, revealing stunning vistas at every turn. [minor] We met wildlife along the way, including deer and bright lizards. [minor] By the time we reached the bottom, we were exhausted but exhilarated. [conclusion]
slides.say: Here is a model narrative with the parts labelled. Last summer, I had an unforgettable adventure at the Kwahu ridge. That is the topic sentence. We hiked down the mountains, which offered breathtaking views and tough terrain. That is the major support. The trail was steep and narrow, revealing stunning vistas at every turn. That is a minor supporting sentence. We met wildlife along the way, including deer and bright lizards. That is another. By the time we reached the bottom, we were exhausted but exhilarated. That is the concluding sentence.
slides.example: Last summer, I had an unforgettable adventure at the Kwahu ridge. We hiked down the mountains, which offered breathtaking views. The trail was steep and narrow, revealing stunning vistas at every turn. By the time we reached the bottom, we were exhausted but exhilarated.

slides.html:
Arrange events in the order they happened: a clear beginning, a middle that develops the story, and an end.

First, they packed their bags. Then, they drove to the beach. In the afternoon, they swam and built sandcastles. Finally, they returned home, tired but happy.

Use time words: first, then, after, finally.
slides.say: Arrange your events in the order they happened: a clear beginning, a middle that develops the story, and an end. For example: First, they packed their bags. Then, they drove to the beach. In the afternoon, they swam and built sandcastles. Finally, they returned home, tired but happy. Use time words, such as: first, then, after, and finally.
slides.example: First, they packed their bags. Then, they drove to the beach. In the afternoon, they swam and built sandcastles. Finally, they returned home, tired but happy.

slides.html:
Characters are the people, animals or places in the story. When you introduce one, give its background and its present situation.

Nestled in the green hills, the village of Nkwanta is where time stands still. It is rich in history… Presently, it blends tradition with a new school and solar panels.
slides.say: Characters are the people, animals, or places in the story. When you introduce a character, give its background, and its present situation. For example: Nestled in the green hills, the village of Nkwanta is where time stands still. It is rich in history. And presently, it blends tradition with a new school, and solar panels. Notice how the character is introduced with both past and present information.
slides.example: Nestled in the green hills, the village of Nkwanta is where time stands still. Once a quiet farming settlement, it now blends old traditions with a new school and solar panels.

slides.html:
Make characters vivid by describing their appearance, actions, thoughts and feelings — much like a descriptive paragraph.

Jenny, a curious 10-year-old girl with curly hair, was excited about her first trip to the beach.
RememberKeep everything in the past tense: was, hiked, found, smiled.
slides.say: Make your characters vivid by describing their appearance, their actions, their thoughts, and their feelings, much like a descriptive paragraph. For example: Jenny, a curious ten year old girl with curly hair, was excited about her first trip to the beach. And remember to keep everything in the past tense: was, hiked, found, and smiled.
slides.example: Jenny, a curious ten-year-old girl with curly hair, was excited about her very first trip to the beach.

slides.html:
The setting is the time and place of the story. It creates context and atmosphere — include the location, weather, time of day and mood.

It was a bright, sunny day on the sandy shores of the beach, with waves crashing against the rocks.
slides.say: The setting is the time and place of the story. It creates context and atmosphere. Include the location, the weather, the time of day, and the mood. For example: It was a bright, sunny day on the sandy shores of the beach, with waves crashing against the rocks.
slides.example: It was a bright, sunny day on the sandy shores of the beach, with gentle waves crashing against the rocks.

slides.html:
The plot is the sequence of events. A full plot has an introduction, rising action, climax, falling action and resolution.

Most plots turn on a conflict (a problem) that is finally resolved.

Jenny lost her toy in the sand, leading to a frantic search. At last she found it under some seashells — making her day even better.
slides.say: The plot is the sequence of events. A full plot has an introduction, rising action, a climax, falling action, and a resolution. Most plots turn on a conflict, which is a problem, that is finally resolved. For example: Jenny lost her toy in the sand, leading to a frantic search. At last, she found it under some seashells, making her day even better.
slides.example: Jenny lost her toy in the sand, which led to a frantic search. At last she found it buried beneath some seashells, making her day even better.

slides.html:
Guide the reader through the story with transitions:

  • Time: first, next, then, after, finally
  • Place: nearby, above, below, beside
  • Cause & effect: because, therefore, as a result
First, they set up the umbrella. Next, they spread their towels. Then, they ran to the water. Finally, they watched the sunset.
slides.say: Guide the reader through your story with transitions. For time: first, next, then, after, and finally. For place: nearby, above, below, and beside. And for cause and effect: because, therefore, and as a result. For example: First, they set up the umbrella. Next, they spread their towels. Then, they ran to the water. Finally, they watched the sunset.
slides.example: First, they set up the umbrella. Next, they spread their towels. Then, they ran to the water. Finally, they watched the sunset together.

slides.html:
Walking through the busy market of Nkwanta, I met Ama, the village herbalist. With her greying hair tied back and her hands stained from years among medicinal plants, she greeted everyone warmly. As she explained the herbs and their uses, it became clear that Ama was not only a healer but a keeper of the village's heritage.

Setting → character → background → mood → progression, all in one paragraph.
slides.say: Here is a character in action. Walking through the busy market of Nkwanta, I met Ama, the village herbalist. With her greying hair tied back, and her hands stained from years among medicinal plants, she greeted everyone warmly. As she explained the herbs and their uses, it became clear that Ama was not only a healer, but a keeper of the village's heritage. Notice the flow: setting, then character, then background, then mood, then progression, all in one paragraph.
slides.example: Walking through the busy market of Nkwanta, I met Ama, the village herbalist. With her greying hair tied back and her hands stained from years among medicinal plants, she greeted everyone with a warm, knowing smile.

slides.html:
Let us recap a narrative paragraph:

  • Parts: topic sentence → major support → minor support → conclusion.
  • Chronological order with time transitions.
  • Characters, setting, plot (with a conflict and resolution).
  • Always in the past tense.
Well doneYou can now tell a clear, vivid story in a single paragraph.
slides.say: Let us recap a narrative paragraph. Its parts are: topic sentence, then major support, then minor support, then a conclusion. It follows chronological order, with time transitions. It has characters, a setting, and a plot, with a conflict and a resolution. And it is always written in the past tense. Well done. You can now tell a clear, vivid story in a single paragraph.
slides.example: A good narrative tells a clear, vivid story in time order, in the past tense, from beginning to end.

rewrite.weak: I go to the beach with my family. We swim. We eat food. It is fun. We go home.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite this as a proper narrative paragraph: use the PAST TENSE, add detail, and use time transitions (first, then, finally).
rewrite.model: Last Saturday, my family and I went to the beach. First, we spread our mats on the warm sand and ran straight into the cool waves. Then, we shared a picnic of jollof rice and fried fish under a shady palm. As the sun began to set, we packed up, tired but happy. It was a day none of us would soon forget.
rubric: The whole paragraph is in the past tense
Vivid details are added (not just plain facts)
It ends with a concluding/reflecting sentence

points.passage: Yesterday, I visited my grandparents' farm in the countryside. The day started with a beautiful sunrise that painted the sky orange and pink. We spent the morning harvesting vegetables, pulling out fresh carrots and gathering ripe tomatoes. After a hearty lunch under a big oak tree, we fed the chickens and collected eggs. By the end of the day, I felt a deep sense of satisfaction at having helped my grandparents.
model: Topic sentence: 'Yesterday, I visited my grandparents' farm in the countryside.'
Major supporting sentence: the day started with a beautiful sunrise
Minor details: harvesting carrots and tomatoes; lunch under the oak; feeding chickens and collecting eggs
Concluding sentence: 'By the end of the day, I felt a deep sense of satisfaction...'

points: Topic sentence: Last weekend, I had an exciting adventure at the forest reserve.
Major event: we followed a guide along a hidden trail
Detail: we spotted monkeys and bright birds
Detail: a sudden rain sent us running for shelter
Ending: we returned muddy but thrilled
frompoints.instruction: Write ONE narrative paragraph from these parts, in the PAST TENSE, with time transitions.
frompoints.model: Last weekend, I had an exciting adventure at the forest reserve. First, we followed a friendly guide along a narrow, hidden trail. Along the way, we spotted playful monkeys swinging overhead and birds in brilliant colours. Then, without warning, a heavy rain sent us laughing and running for shelter under a giant tree. By the time it cleared, we returned to the gate muddy but thrilled, full of stories to tell.
rubric: Events follow time order with transitions
All parts are in the past tense

question.q: Write a narrative paragraph (5–7 sentences) about a memorable day. Use the past tense, time transitions, and include a topic sentence, details and a concluding sentence.
question.model: The day I learned to ride a bicycle is one I will never forget. Early that morning, my elder brother wheeled out his old blue bicycle and promised to teach me. At first, I wobbled and fell more times than I could count, scraping my knee on the rough ground. But my brother held the seat and ran beside me, shouting encouragement. Then, almost by surprise, he let go, and I was riding on my own. By evening, I was circling the compound proudly, certain I could do anything I set my mind to.
rubric: Opens with a clear topic sentence
Written entirely in the past tense
Ends with a concluding or reflecting sentence

title: Expository Paragraphs
blurb: Inform and explain clearly: concise facts, an objective tone, and a logical order.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Explain what an expository paragraph is.
  • Write clear, concise, factual information.
  • Keep an objective tone and arrange ideas in a logical order.
slides.say: Welcome. Here are our objectives. By the end of this module, you will be able to explain what an expository paragraph is. You will write clear, concise, factual information. And you will keep an objective tone, and arrange your ideas in a logical order.
slides.example: An expository paragraph informs or explains a topic clearly, factually and objectively.

slides.html:
An expository paragraph gives information or explains a topic. It is factual and objective — its job is to deliver clear, accurate information, not to entertain or persuade.

ExamplesHow photosynthesis works · how to protect water bodies · the importance of food.
slides.say: An expository paragraph gives information, or explains a topic. It is factual and objective. Its job is to deliver clear, accurate information, not to entertain or to persuade. Examples include: how photosynthesis works, how to protect water bodies, or the importance of food.
slides.example: An expository paragraph gives information or explains a topic in a clear, factual way.

slides.html:
Deliver facts plainly. Three habits help:

  • Avoid jargon — use simple, precise words.
  • Focus on facts — no digressions.
  • Be specific — use concrete examples and figures.
Many people suffer from dehydration.
According to the WHO, about 1 in 10 people worldwide suffer from dehydration due to inadequate water intake.
slides.say: Deliver your facts plainly. Three habits help. Avoid jargon: use simple, precise words. Focus on facts: no digressions. And be specific: use concrete examples and figures. Compare a vague version: many people suffer from dehydration. With a specific one: according to the World Health Organization, about one in ten people worldwide suffer from dehydration, due to inadequate water intake.
slides.example: According to the World Health Organization, about one in ten people worldwide suffer from dehydration due to inadequate water intake.

slides.html:
