Present information impartially — no personal bias, emotions or opinions.

  • Avoid subjectivity — no I think, in my opinion.
  • Use neutral language.
  • Present multiple sides where it applies.
I believe everyone should exercise because it's good for you.
Research indicates that regular exercise reduces the risk of heart disease, diabetes and obesity.
slides.say: Present your information impartially, with no personal bias, emotions, or opinions. Avoid subjectivity: do not write, I think, or, in my opinion. Use neutral language. And present multiple sides where it applies. Compare a subjective sentence: I believe everyone should exercise because it's good for you. With an objective one: research indicates that regular exercise reduces the risk of heart disease, diabetes, and obesity.
slides.example: Research indicates that regular exercise reduces the risk of heart disease, diabetes and obesity.

slides.html:
Organise ideas so each flows into the next. Common patterns:

  • Chronological — steps or events in time order.
  • Order of importance — most to least important.
  • Cause & effect — how one thing leads to another.
  • Comparison & contrast — similarities and differences.
slides.say: Organise your ideas so that each one flows into the next. Common patterns are: chronological order, which is steps or events in time order. Order of importance, from most to least important. Cause and effect, showing how one thing leads to another. And comparison and contrast, showing similarities and differences.
slides.example: First, sunlight is absorbed by chlorophyll. Next, it is used to make glucose. Finally, oxygen is released into the air.

slides.html:
Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants make their food. It happens in the chloroplasts, where chlorophyll absorbs sunlight. That light energy turns carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen. The plant uses the glucose for energy and growth, while the oxygen is released into the air. This process is essential for plants and for the oxygen other living things need.

Clear, factual, objective — and arranged in a logical, step-by-step order.
slides.say: Here is a model expository paragraph. Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants make their food. It happens in the chloroplasts, where chlorophyll absorbs sunlight. That light energy turns carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen. The plant uses the glucose for energy and growth, while the oxygen is released into the air. This process is essential for plants, and for the oxygen that other living things need. Notice: it is clear, factual, and objective, and arranged in a logical, step by step order.
slides.example: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants make their food. It happens in the chloroplasts, where chlorophyll absorbs sunlight. That light energy turns carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen. The plant uses the glucose for energy, while the oxygen is released into the air.

slides.html:
Let us recap the expository paragraph:

  • It informs or explains — factual and objective.
  • Clear & concise: no jargon, focus on facts, be specific.
  • Objective tone: no personal opinion; neutral language.
  • Logical order: chronological, importance, cause/effect or compare/contrast.
Well doneYou can now explain any topic clearly and fairly.
slides.say: Let us recap the expository paragraph. It informs, or explains, and it is factual and objective. Keep it clear and concise: no jargon, focus on facts, and be specific. Keep an objective tone: no personal opinion, and neutral language. And use a logical order: chronological, importance, cause and effect, or comparison and contrast. Well done. You can now explain any topic clearly, and fairly.
slides.example: An expository paragraph explains a topic clearly, in an objective tone and a logical order.

rewrite.weak: I think everyone should drink more water because honestly dehydration is really bad and water is just so good for you.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite this as an OBJECTIVE, factual expository sentence or two. Remove opinion words and add a specific fact.
rewrite.model: Adequate water intake is essential for health. According to the World Health Organization, about 1 in 10 people worldwide suffer from dehydration due to insufficient water, which can impair concentration and organ function.
rubric: No opinion phrases remain (I think/honestly/so good)
The tone is neutral and factual
A specific fact or figure is included
The information is clear and concise

points.passage: A balanced diet is essential for maintaining good health. It provides the body with the nutrients, vitamins and minerals it needs to function properly. By eating a variety of foods, you can ensure that your body gets everything it needs to stay healthy and resist disease.
model: Main idea: a balanced diet is essential for good health
Mode of writing: Expository (it informs/explains)
Purpose: to explain why a balanced diet matters
Audience: learners / general public

frompoints.instruction: Write ONE objective, factual expository paragraph from these points, in a logical order.
frompoints.model: Festivals offer several important benefits to a community. First, they help preserve culture and traditions by passing customs from one generation to the next. Secondly, they bring people together, strengthening unity and a shared sense of identity. Finally, festivals boost the local economy, as visitors spend money on food, lodging and trade. In these ways, festivals serve both cultural and economic purposes.
rubric: The tone stays objective (no personal opinion)
Ideas follow a logical order with transitions
The language is clear and specific

question.q: Write an EXPOSITORY paragraph explaining three ways of protecting water bodies in the country. Keep it factual and objective.
question.model: Protecting our water bodies requires deliberate action on several fronts. First, factories and households should be stopped from dumping waste and chemicals into rivers, as this pollutes the water supply. Secondly, illegal mining near rivers, known as galamsey, must be controlled, since it fills the water with mud and harmful metals. Finally, planting trees along riverbanks helps prevent erosion and keeps the water clean. Together, these measures can preserve the country's water bodies for future generations.
rubric: The tone is objective and factual (no opinion)
Ideas are in a logical order with transitions

title: Persuasive Paragraphs
blurb: Convince your reader: a clear stance, logical reasoning with evidence, and an emotional appeal.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Explain what a persuasive paragraph does.
  • State a clear argument in your topic sentence.
  • Support it with logical reasoning + evidence and an emotional appeal.
slides.say: Welcome. Here are our objectives. By the end of this module, you will be able to explain what a persuasive paragraph does. You will state a clear argument in your topic sentence. And you will support it with logical reasoning and evidence, plus an emotional appeal.
slides.example: A persuasive paragraph convinces the reader using a clear stance, evidence and an emotional appeal.

slides.html:
A persuasive paragraph aims to convince the reader of a point of view, or to take an action. It blends logic, reasoning and emotional appeals.

Unlike expositoryExpository stays neutral; persuasive takes a side and argues for it.
slides.say: A persuasive paragraph aims to convince the reader of a point of view, or to take an action. It blends logic, reasoning, and emotional appeals. Unlike an expository paragraph, which stays neutral, a persuasive paragraph takes a side, and argues for it.
slides.example: A persuasive paragraph aims to convince the reader of a point of view or to take an action.

slides.html:
State your position clearly — usually in the topic sentence. It sets the direction for the whole paragraph.

Implementing Free SHS will open access to education for greater minds in the country.
School uniforms should be mandatory for all students.
slides.say: State your position clearly, usually in the topic sentence. It sets the direction for the whole paragraph. For example: Implementing Free S H S will open access to education for greater minds in the country. Or: School uniforms should be mandatory for all students.
slides.example: School uniforms should be mandatory for all students.

slides.html:
Back your stance with reasons and evidence — facts, statistics, expert findings and examples.

A University of Ghana study found employees working four days a week were 25% more productive; MTN reported a 40% boost after a similar change.
slides.say: Back your stance with reasons, and evidence: facts, statistics, expert findings, and examples. For example: A University of Ghana study found that employees working four days a week were twenty five percent more productive; and M T N reported a forty percent boost after a similar change.
slides.example: A University of Ghana study found that employees working four days a week were twenty-five percent more productive.

slides.html:
Connect with the reader's feelings and values — empathy, hope, excitement or concern. This makes the argument land.

Imagine the boost to your mental health and family life with an extra day off each week — more time to rest, and to be with those you love.
slides.say: Connect with the reader's feelings and values: empathy, hope, excitement, or concern. This makes your argument land. For example: Imagine the boost to your mental health and family life, with an extra day off each week, more time to rest, and to be with those you love.
slides.example: Imagine the boost to your health and family life with an extra day off each week, more time to rest and to be with those you love.

slides.html: School uniforms should be mandatory for all students. [stance] They create equality and reduce bullying based on clothing, and they build school pride and unity. [reasons] They also save families time and money, since parents need not buy many outfits. [evidence/benefit] Imagine a calmer school where every child feels they belong. [emotional appeal] Therefore, a uniform policy leads to a more focused, united school.
slides.say: Here is a model persuasive paragraph. School uniforms should be mandatory for all students. That is the stance. They create equality and reduce bullying based on clothing, and they build school pride and unity. Those are the reasons. They also save families time and money, since parents need not buy many outfits. That is a supporting benefit. Imagine a calmer school where every child feels they belong. That is the emotional appeal. Therefore, a uniform policy leads to a more focused, united school.
slides.example: School uniforms should be mandatory for all students. They create equality and reduce bullying based on clothing, and they build school pride. They also save families time and money. Imagine a calmer school where every child feels they belong. A uniform policy leads to a more focused, united school.

slides.html:
Let us recap the persuasive paragraph:

  • It aims to convince the reader of a view or action.
  • Clear stance in the topic sentence.
  • Logical reasoning + evidence (facts, figures, examples).
  • At least one emotional appeal.
Well doneYou can now argue a case clearly and convincingly.
slides.say: Let us recap the persuasive paragraph. It aims to convince the reader of a view, or an action. It states a clear stance in the topic sentence. It uses logical reasoning and evidence, such as facts, figures, and examples. And it includes at least one emotional appeal. Well done. You can now argue a case clearly, and convincingly.
slides.example: A persuasive paragraph states a clear stance, supports it with evidence, and adds an emotional appeal.

rewrite.weak: Recycling is okay. Some people do it. It is good for the earth I guess.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite this as a PERSUASIVE paragraph: a clear stance, a reason with evidence, and one emotional appeal.
rewrite.model: Recycling should be mandatory in every household. It cuts the waste piling up in our landfills, conserves natural resources, and saves energy. Studies show that recycling one tonne of paper can save many trees and thousands of litres of water. Imagine leaving our children a cleaner, greener country — that future begins with the bin in your kitchen.
rubric: A clear stance is stated up front
At least one reason with supporting evidence

points.passage: Adopting a plant-based diet can significantly improve your health. Numerous studies show that reducing meat lowers the risk of heart disease, diabetes and certain cancers. Plant-based diets are also rich in fibre, vitamins and antioxidants. Imagine the energy you'll have eating clean, wholesome foods, while helping to protect the planet.
model: Topic sentence / stance: a plant-based diet can improve your health
Logical reasoning & evidence: studies show lower risk of heart disease, diabetes, cancers; rich in fibre/vitamins
Emotional appeal: 'Imagine the energy you'll have...' and helping the planet
Mode: persuasive (it argues a position)

points: Stance: pets are good for the household
Reason: they teach responsibility and routine
Evidence: studies link pets to lower stress
Emotional appeal: the joy and companionship a pet brings
frompoints.instruction: Write ONE persuasive paragraph from these points: clear stance → reasoning with evidence → an emotional appeal.
frompoints.model: Every household would benefit from having a pet. Caring for an animal teaches children responsibility and a daily routine, as they learn to feed and look after another life. Research has also linked pet ownership to lower stress and blood pressure. Above all, imagine the warm companionship of a dog greeting you at the door — a joy that brightens even the hardest day. For these reasons, a pet is a worthy addition to any home.

question.q: Write a PERSUASIVE paragraph on why schools should have longer recess periods. Include a clear stance, logical reasoning with evidence, and at least one emotional appeal.
question.model: Schools should give students longer recess periods. Longer breaks let children move, play and reset, which research shows improves concentration and behaviour back in class. In one study, pupils with more break time scored higher in afternoon lessons. Think of the bright, energetic classroom that follows happy children who have run and laughed in the sun. For healthier, sharper learners, schools should lengthen recess.
rubric: A clear stance opens the paragraph
At least one piece of evidence/example
Ends with a persuasive closing statement

title: Statistics & Graphs
blurb: Back up your claims with facts and figures — and present them with tables, bar graphs and pie charts.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Explain why statistics strengthen an essay.
  • State a figure with its source and date, then link it to your point.
  • Present data using a table, bar graph or pie chart.
slides.say: Welcome. Here are our objectives. By the end of this module, you will be able to explain why statistics strengthen an essay. You will state a figure with its source and date, then link it to your point. And you will present data using a table, a bar graph, or a pie chart.
slides.example: Statistics, tables and graphs make a claim verifiable and convincing.

slides.html:
Statistics are facts and figures that explain a claim. They make a claim verifiably true, which is powerful in argumentative essays and speeches.

NoteSome essays cannot use figures (e.g. some social-studies topics); some allow you to create your own to show creativity.
slides.say: Statistics are facts and figures that explain a claim. They make a claim verifiably true, which is powerful in argumentative essays, and speeches. A note: some essays cannot use figures, such as certain social studies topics. And some essays allow you to create your own figures, simply to show creativity.
slides.example: Statistics are facts and figures that make a claim verifiably true.

slides.html:
The source matters — it lets your figures be verified. Common sources:

  • the headmaster's office; the library
  • popular books; search engines (the internet)
  • government and statistical departments
  • your own personal research
slides.say: The source matters, because it lets your figures be verified. Common sources include: the headmaster's office; the library; popular books; search engines, that is, the internet; government and statistical departments; and your own personal research.
slides.example: Reliable figures come from sources such as government departments, libraries and official reports.

slides.html:
Insert figures where they prove the weight or intensity of a claim — at the cause, result, effect, instance or solution.

Best placePut them where most of your supporting details are, and where readers need to feel how serious the issue is.
slides.say: Insert your figures where they prove the weight, or the intensity, of a claim, that is, at the cause, the result, the effect, an instance, or the solution. The best place is where most of your supporting details are, and where readers need to feel how serious the issue is.
slides.example: Place your figures where the supporting details are, to show the reader how serious the issue is.

slides.html:
State the source first, or the figure first then the source. Always include the date.

According to the Ministry of Health report (2010), about 10,000 teenage girls die each year from unsafe abortion.
About 10,000 teenage girls die from unsafe abortion each year (Ministry of Health, 2010).
slides.say: State the source first, or state the figure first and then the source. And always include the date. For example: According to the Ministry of Health report, 2010, about ten thousand teenage girls die each year from unsafe abortion. Or: About ten thousand teenage girls die from unsafe abortion each year, source, Ministry of Health, 2010.
slides.example: According to the Ministry of Health report (2010), about 10,000 teenage girls die each year from unsafe abortion.

slides.h: Link the statistic to your essay
slides.html:
Never leave a figure hanging. After it, you must:

  • Infer — say what the figure means.
  • Forecast — predict the likely trend if nothing changes.
This shows that teenage pregnancy is rising every year. If care is not taken, I fear these figures will double next year.
slides.say: Never leave a figure hanging. After it, you must do two things. Infer: say what the figure means. And forecast: predict the likely trend if nothing changes. For example: This shows that teenage pregnancy is rising every year. If care is not taken, I fear these figures will double next year.
slides.example: This shows that teenage pregnancy is rising every year. If care is not taken, I fear these figures will double next year.

slides.html:
When you have many figures, a table makes them easier to read. Steps:

  • Make columns and rows for items, periods and figures.
  • Give the source below the table.
  • Link a sentence of explanation to the table.
From the table above, performance is poor — the average pass is below 10%. If unchecked, this is likely to continue next year.
slides.say: When you have many figures, a table makes them easier to read. Follow these steps. Make columns and rows for the items, the periods, and the figures. Give the source below the table. And link a sentence of explanation to the table. For example: From the table above, performance is poor, as the average pass is below ten percent. If unchecked, this is likely to continue next year.
slides.example: From the table above, performance is poor, as the average pass is below ten percent. If this is not checked, it is likely to continue next year.

slides.html:
Graphs add visual proof and show your essay is factual.

  • Bar graph — compares quantities (e.g. boys vs girls each year).
  • Pie chart — shows shares of a whole (e.g. the failure rate by year).

Always name the graph, say what it shows, then interpret it as you would a table.
slides.say: Graphs add visual proof, and show that your essay is factual. A bar graph compares quantities, for example, boys versus girls each year. A pie chart shows shares of a whole, for example, the failure rate by year. Always name the graph, say what it shows, then interpret it, just as you would a table.
slides.example: The bar graph above shows that pass rates fell steadily from 2010 to 2013 for both boys and girls.

slides.html:
Let us recap:

  • Statistics make claims verifiable — strongest in argument essays.
  • Always give the source and date.
  • Link every figure: infer its meaning, then forecast the trend.
  • Use a table for many figures; a bar graph to compare; a pie chart for shares.
Well doneYour essays can now prove their claims with solid evidence.
slides.say: Let us recap. Statistics make your claims verifiable, and they are strongest in argument essays. Always give the source and the date. Link every figure: infer its meaning, then forecast the trend. And use a table for many figures, a bar graph to compare, and a pie chart for shares. Well done. Your essays can now prove their claims with solid evidence.
slides.example: Always give a figure with its source and date, then interpret it and forecast the trend.

rewrite.weak: Many students fail the exam every year and it is really bad.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite this to include a statistic with a source and date, then link it (infer + forecast).
rewrite.model: According to the headmaster's BECE report (2015), only 1 of the 150 candidates passed all six subjects. This shows that the school's performance is very poor, and if nothing changes, I fear the results will be worse next year.
rubric: A source and date are given
The figure is interpreted (what it means)
A forecast of the trend is added

points.passage: According to the Ministry of Health report (2010), about 10,000 teenage girls die each year from unsafe abortion. This shows that teenage pregnancy is rising sharply. If care is not taken, I fear these figures will double next year.
model: The statistic: about 10,000 teenage girls die each year from unsafe abortion
The source and date: Ministry of Health report, 2010
The inference: teenage pregnancy is rising sharply
The forecast: the figures may double next year if nothing changes

points: Claim: the school's BECE performance is poor
Figure: out of 120 candidates in 2009, only 50 passed all six subjects
frompoints.instruction: Write 2–3 sentences using this statistic correctly: state it with the source, then infer and forecast.
frompoints.model: According to the headmaster's BECE report (2009), out of the 120 candidates the school presented, only 50 passed all six subjects. This shows that the school's performance is weak. Unless the authorities act, this poor trend is likely to continue in the coming years.
rubric: The source and date are included
An inference explains what it means

question.q: Write a short paragraph arguing that road accidents are a serious problem, supporting your claim with a statistic (state a source and date, then infer and forecast).
question.model: Road accidents have become a serious danger on our highways. According to the National Road Safety Authority report (2014), over 1,800 people died in road crashes in a single year. This shows that careless driving and poor roads are claiming lives at an alarming rate. If urgent steps are not taken, I fear these deaths will keep rising in the years ahead.
rubric: A clear claim/topic sentence opens it
A statistic is included with a source and date
A forecast of the trend is given

title: Essay Structure
blurb: Build a full essay: two ways to write the introduction, a developed main body, and a strong conclusion.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Name the three parts of an essay.
  • Write an introduction by the definition method and the general background method.
  • Develop a strong main body paragraph and a clear conclusion.
slides.say: Welcome. Here are our objectives. By the end of this module, you will name the three parts of an essay. You will write an introduction by the definition method, and by the general background method. And you will develop a strong main body paragraph, and a clear conclusion.
slides.example: An essay has three parts: an introduction, a body, and a conclusion.

slides.html:
An essay is like the human body — three parts joined together:

  • Introduction (the head)
  • Body (the middle)
  • Conclusion (the legs)

An essay can be a letter, an argument, a speech, a description or a story — but it always has these parts.
slides.say: An essay is like the human body, with three parts joined together. The introduction, which is the head. The body, which is the middle. And the conclusion, which is the legs. An essay can be a letter, an argument, a speech, a description, or a story, but it always has these three parts.
slides.example: An essay is like the human body: it has a head (introduction), a body, and legs (conclusion).

slides.html:
The introduction is a brief summary of the whole essay, written in one paragraph. It has three parts:

  • The topic — what the question asks you to write about.
  • The sub-topics (points) — the answers that make the topic true.
  • The readiness — a line signalling the details are coming.
slides.say: The introduction is a brief summary of the whole essay, written in one paragraph. It has three parts. The topic, which is what the question asks you to write about. The sub-topics, or points, which are the answers that make the topic true. And the readiness, which is a line signalling that the details are coming.
slides.example: A good introduction states the topic, gives the points, and shows that the details are coming.

slides.html:
Write your sub-points in two moves:

  • Prepare the background: There are several [topic].
  • Bring out the points: Some of the [topic] are: …, … and … .
There are several causes of teenage pregnancy. Some of the causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence.

Points must be nouns or noun phrases — no subjects, no negations.
slides.say: Write your sub-points in two moves. First, prepare the background, by saying: There are several of the topic. Then bring out the points, by saying: Some of the topic are, and list them. For example: There are several causes of teenage pregnancy. Some of the causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Remember, points must be nouns or noun phrases, with no subjects, and no negations.
slides.example: There are several causes of teenage pregnancy. Some of the causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence.

slides.h: Two ways to write the introduction
slides.html:
For normal essays (e.g. Social Studies, R.M.E.), there are two ways to open:

  • Definition method — define the key term first.
  • General background method — give background facts first.

Both then give the points and show readiness. Let us look at each.
slides.say: For normal essays, such as in Social Studies or Religious and Moral Education, there are two ways to open. The definition method, where you define the key term first. And the general background method, where you give background facts first. Both then give the points, and show readiness. Let us look at each.
slides.example: An introduction can open with the definition method or the general background method.

slides.html:
Steps:

  • Define the key term or topic.
  • Bring out the points.
  • State readiness.
Agriculture is the rearing of farm animals and growing of crops for human benefit. There are several importance of agriculture. Some of the importance are the provision of food, revenue to government, and sports. The details of the importance of agriculture are below.
slides.say: Here are the steps for the definition method. First, define the key term, or the topic. Then bring out the points. Then state readiness. For example: Agriculture is the rearing of farm animals, and the growing of crops, for human benefit. There are several importance of agriculture. Some of the importance are the provision of food, revenue to government, and sports. The details of the importance of agriculture are below.
slides.example: Agriculture is the rearing of farm animals and the growing of crops for human benefit. There are several importance of agriculture. Some of them are the provision of food, revenue to government, and employment. The details are below.

slides.html:
Steps:

  • State a general background about the topic.
  • Bring out the points.
  • Show readiness.
Agriculture is the backbone of Ghana's economy, contributing about 50% of the country's GDP. The importance of agriculture to the nation are numerous. Some of them are the provision of food, revenue generation, and sports. The details of the importance are below.
slides.say: Here are the steps for the general background method. First, state a general background about the topic. Then bring out the points. Then show readiness. For example: Agriculture is the backbone of Ghana's economy, contributing about fifty percent of the country's G D P. The importance of agriculture to the nation are numerous. Some of them are the provision of food, revenue generation, and sports. The details of the importance are below.
slides.example: Agriculture is the backbone of Ghana's economy, contributing about fifty percent of the country's GDP. The importance of agriculture are numerous. Some of them are the provision of food, revenue, and employment. The details are below.

slides.h: The main body — one point per paragraph
slides.html:
Each body paragraph develops one point from your introduction, taken in the same order. A full body paragraph has:

  • Topic sentence (the point) → major support (restate simply)
  • Minor support (details) → example → result → effect → solution
ReminderYou learned this fully in Parts of a Paragraph (Module 4).
slides.say: Each body paragraph develops one point from your introduction, taken in the same order. A full body paragraph has: a topic sentence, which is the point; a major support, which restates it simply; minor support, which gives the details; then an example, the result, the effect, and a solution. You learned all of this fully in the Parts of a Paragraph module.
slides.example: Each body paragraph develops one point: topic sentence, support, example, result, effect, and solution.

slides.html: The first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. [topic] Some parents do not take good care of their children. [major] They fail to provide food, shelter and clothing, and give no moral training, because they are too busy working. [minor] For instance, many children sleep on the streets at Kejetia. [example] The result is that they seek their needs elsewhere, and the effect is that many girls fall pregnant. [result/effect] Parents are therefore advised to care properly for their children. [solution]
slides.say: Here is a model body paragraph. The first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. That is the topic sentence. Some parents do not take good care of their children. That is the major support. They fail to provide food, shelter and clothing, and give no moral training, because they are too busy working. Those are the minor supporting details. For instance, many children sleep on the streets at Kejetia. That is the example. The result is that they seek their needs elsewhere, and the effect is that many girls fall pregnant. Those are the result and effect. Parents are therefore advised to care properly for their children. That is the solution.
slides.example: The first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. Many parents do not provide food, shelter or moral guidance because they are busy working. For instance, some children sleep on the streets at Kejetia. The result is that they seek their needs elsewhere, and the effect is that many girls fall pregnant. Parents should therefore care properly for their children.

slides.html:
The conclusion is a definite remark that makes your essay stand. Two moves:

  • Summary of the points — restate them briefly.
  • Final statement — your definite stand, a recommendation, or a call to action.
In conclusion, the causes of teenage pregnancy are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. These problems must be tackled to protect our young people.
slides.say: The conclusion is a definite remark that makes your essay stand. It has two moves. First, a summary of the points, where you restate them briefly. Second, a final statement, which is your definite stand, a recommendation, or a call to action. For example: In conclusion, the causes of teenage pregnancy are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. These problems must be tackled, to protect our young people.
slides.example: In conclusion, the causes of teenage pregnancy are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. These problems must be tackled to protect our young people.

slides.html:
Let us recap the essay:

  • Three parts: introduction, body, conclusion.
  • Introduction: topic → points → readiness, opened by the definition or general background method.
  • Body: one developed point per paragraph (topic → support → example → result → effect → solution).
  • Conclusion: summary of points + a final statement.
Well doneYou now have a complete, reliable formula for any essay.
slides.say: Let us recap the essay. It has three parts: introduction, body, and conclusion. For the introduction: topic, then points, then readiness, opened by either the definition method, or the general background method. For the body: one developed point per paragraph, going from topic sentence, to support, to example, to result, to effect, to solution. And the conclusion gives a summary of the points, plus a final statement. Well done. You now have a complete, reliable formula for any essay.
slides.example: An essay flows from a clear introduction, through a well-developed body, to a firm conclusion.

rewrite.weak: Agriculture is important. It gives us food. Government gets money. There is sports.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite this as a proper introduction using the DEFINITION method: define agriculture, give the points as noun phrases, then show readiness.
rewrite.model: Agriculture is the rearing of farm animals and the growing of crops for human benefit. There are several importance of agriculture. Some of the importance are the provision of food, revenue to government, and sports. The details of the importance of agriculture are below.
rubric: Opens by defining the key term (agriculture)
Points are stated as noun phrases
Ends with a readiness line ('The details ... are below')

points.passage: Agriculture is the backbone of Ghana's economy, contributing about 50% of the country's GDP. The importance of agriculture to the nation are numerous. Some of them are the provision of food, revenue generation, and sports. The details of the importance are below.
model: Method used: general background method (opens with a background fact)
Background: agriculture is the backbone of the economy (~50% of GDP)
The points: provision of food; revenue generation; sports
Readiness line: 'The details of the importance are below.'

frompoints.instruction: Write the INTRODUCTION using the DEFINITION method: define education, give the points, then show readiness.
frompoints.model: Education is the process of gaining knowledge, skills and good values through teaching and learning. There are several importance of education. Some of the importance are the provision of knowledge and skills, the reduction of poverty, and national development. The details of the importance of education are below.
rubric: Begins by defining the key term

question.q: Write the INTRODUCTION (general background method), ONE body paragraph, and the CONCLUSION of an essay on the importance of agriculture.
question.model:
INTRODUCTION: Agriculture is the backbone of Ghana's economy, contributing about half of the country's GDP. The importance of agriculture to the nation are numerous. Some of them are the provision of food, revenue to government, and employment. The details of the importance of agriculture are below.

BODY: The first importance of agriculture is the provision of food. Farmers grow the crops and rear the animals that feed the entire nation. They cultivate staples such as maize, cassava and rice, and raise cattle and poultry. For instance, the markets of Kumasi and Accra are stocked daily with produce from local farms. The result is that families are fed and hunger is reduced. Agriculture should therefore be supported with modern tools and irrigation.

CONCLUSION: In conclusion, the importance of agriculture includes the provision of food, revenue to government, and employment. The nation must give farming the support it deserves so that this vital sector can thrive.
rubric: Introduction uses the general background method (background → points → readiness)
Body develops one point fully (topic → support → example → result → solution)
Conclusion ends with a final statement
The whole piece follows intro → body → conclusion order

title: Polish & Power
blurb: Linking words, vocabulary, editing and the habits of strong writers.

slides.html:
Linking words guide the reader from one idea to the next. Keep a mental toolbox:

  • Adding: also, in addition, furthermore, moreover
  • Contrast: however, on the other hand, although
  • Result: therefore, as a result, consequently
  • Sequence: first, next, then, finally
  • Example: for example, for instance, such as
slides.say: Linking words guide your reader from one idea to the next. Keep a mental toolbox. For adding ideas: also, in addition, furthermore, moreover. For contrast: however, on the other hand, although. For showing a result: therefore, as a result, consequently. For sequence: first, next, then, finally. And for giving an example: for example, for instance, and such as.
slides.example: First, clear the gutters; in addition, stop the dumping of refuse; finally, the flooding will reduce.

slides.html:
Replace weak, overused words with vivid ones.

The food was very nice and the place was very good.
The food was delicious and the restaurant was welcoming.
Watch out for'very', 'nice', 'good', 'things', 'a lot'. They are weak. Ask: what exactly do I mean?
slides.say: Replace weak, overused words with vivid ones. Compare: the food was very nice and the place was very good. Now compare the stronger version: the food was delicious and the restaurant was welcoming. Watch out for weak words like very, nice, good, things, and a lot. When you catch them, ask yourself, what exactly do I mean?
slides.example: The food was delicious and the restaurant was warm and welcoming.

slides.html:
If every sentence is the same length and shape, writing feels flat. Mix it up:

I went home. I ate food. I did homework. I slept.
After a long day, I went home and ate. Once my homework was finished, I fell asleep almost at once.

Combine short ideas, and open some sentences with a linking word or phrase.
slides.say: If every sentence is the same length and shape, your writing feels flat. Mix it up. Compare the flat version: I went home. I ate food. I did homework. I slept. Now the varied version: After a long day, I went home and ate. Once my homework was finished, I fell asleep almost at once. To do this, combine short ideas, and open some sentences with a linking word or phrase.
slides.example: I went home, ate a quick meal, finished my homework, and finally fell asleep.

slides.html:
• their / there / they're — possession / place / they are
  • your / you're — possession / you are
  • its / it's — possession / it is
  • to / too / two — direction / also / number
TrickIf you can replace it with 'it is', write it's. Otherwise write its.
slides.say: Here are common mistakes to fix. Their, there, and they're: one shows possession, one shows place, and one means they are. Your versus you're: one is possession, one means you are. Its versus it's: one is possession, one means it is. And to, too, two: one shows direction, one means also, and one is the number. Here is a trick. If you can replace the word with it is, then write it apostrophe s. Otherwise, write its with no apostrophe.
slides.example: It's raining heavily, and the dog wagged its tail by the door.

slides.html:
Great writing is rewritten. After your first draft, read again and check:

  • Sense — does every sentence make sense?
  • Spelling and punctuation
  • Agreement — subjects and verbs
  • Repetition — cut repeated words and ideas
Power tipRead your work aloud. Your ear catches mistakes your eye misses.
slides.say: Great writing is rewritten. After your first draft, read it again and check four things. Sense: does every sentence make sense? Spelling and punctuation. Agreement: do your subjects and verbs match? And repetition: cut repeated words and ideas. Here is a power tip. Read your work aloud. Your ear catches mistakes that your eye misses.
slides.example: After writing, I read my work aloud, fixed two spelling errors, and added a missing full stop.

slides.html:
The best way to improve your writing is to read widely. When you read good writing, you absorb:

  • new words and how they are used
  • how sentences are built
  • how ideas are organised

Read newspapers, stories, and textbooks. Keep a notebook of new words you meet.
slides.say: The best way to improve your writing is to read widely. When you read good writing, you absorb new words and how they are used, how sentences are built, and how ideas are organised. So read newspapers, stories, and textbooks. And keep a notebook of new words you meet, so you can use them later.
slides.example: The more I read good books, the more naturally strong sentences come to my own writing.

slides.html:
Good spelling builds trust with your reader. Try these:

  • Syllables — break a hard word into parts: gov-ern-ment.
  • Look–cover–write–check — study a word, hide it, write it, then check.
  • Word families — learn groups: nation, national, international.
HabitKeep a list of words you often misspell and review it weekly.
slides.say: Good spelling builds trust with your reader. Try these strategies. First, syllables: break a hard word into parts, like gov, ern, ment, government. Second, look, cover, write, check: study a word, hide it, write it from memory, then check it. Third, word families: learn words in groups, like nation, national, and international. And make this a habit. Keep a list of words you often misspell, and review it every week.
slides.example: I keep a small notebook of tricky words, such as 'necessary' and 'definitely', and review it each week.

slides.html:
Good writers adjust their tone to suit the reader and the purpose.

  • Formal — letters to officials, exams: I am writing to request…
  • Informal — a friend: Hey, guess what happened!
Avoidslang and short forms (e.g. gonna, u) in formal writing.
slides.say: Good writers adjust their tone to suit the reader and the purpose. Use a formal tone for letters to officials, and for exams, for example, I am writing to request. Use an informal tone with a friend, for example, hey, guess what happened! And avoid slang and short forms, like gonna, or the letter u for you, in any formal writing.
slides.example: I would be grateful if you could consider my request and reply at your earliest convenience.

slides.html:
• Did I plan before writing?
  • Clear introduction, body, conclusion?
  • One idea per paragraph?
  • Linking words for flow?
  • Strong, varied sentences and words?
  • Did I proofread aloud?
Congratulations!You now have the full toolkit. Keep practising — every great writer started exactly where you are now.
slides.say: Here is your final writer's checklist. Did I plan before writing? Do I have a clear introduction, body, and conclusion? Is there one idea per paragraph? Have I used linking words for flow? Are my sentences and words strong and varied? And did I proofread aloud? Congratulations. You now have the full toolkit. Keep practising. Every great writer started exactly where you are now.
slides.example: Good writing is clear, correct, varied, and carefully proofread before it is shared.

rewrite.weak: The party was very nice. There was a lot of food. The food was good. People were happy. It was a good day.
rewrite.hint: Replace weak words (very, nice, good, a lot), and vary the sentences.
rewrite.model: The party was unforgettable. Tables overflowed with steaming jollof, grilled chicken and chilled drinks. As music filled the air, guests laughed and danced late into the evening. It was a day none of us would soon forget.
rubric: Weak words (very, nice, good, a lot) are replaced
At least one vivid, specific detail is added
Sentence lengths and openings are varied
At least one linking word/phrase is used

points.passage: There are many reasons why honesty is the best policy. First, honest people earn the trust of others, and trust is the foundation of every good relationship. Furthermore, telling the truth saves us from the stress of remembering lies. However, honesty is not always easy, especially when the truth may upset someone. Nevertheless, in the long run, an honest person enjoys a clear conscience and a good reputation.
model: Main idea: honesty is the best policy
Honest people earn others' trust
Telling the truth avoids the stress of lies
Honesty can be difficult (a fair point/contrast)
In the long run it brings a clear conscience and good name

points: A flat sentence: 'The journey was long. The bus was hot. I was tired.'
frompoints.instruction: Rewrite these three flat sentences into ONE or TWO flowing sentences. Use linking words and stronger vocabulary.
frompoints.model: The journey felt endless, and because the bus was stuffy and overcrowded, I arrived utterly exhausted.
rubric: The choppy sentences are combined smoothly
At least one linking word (and/because/so) is used
A stronger word replaces a weak one (e.g. 'hot'→'stuffy')
The new version reads naturally aloud

question.q: Write a short paragraph (4–6 sentences) about 'My ambition in life.' Then EDIT it: underline any weak words in your mind, add at least two linking words, and make sure your sentences vary in length.
question.model: My greatest ambition is to become a medical doctor. Ever since I nursed my younger sister through a long illness, I have dreamed of healing the sick. To achieve this, I study science seriously and read about the human body whenever I can. Although the path is long and difficult, I am determined to succeed. In the end, I hope to serve my community and make my family proud.
rubric: A clear ambition is stated in the first sentence
At least one reason or example explains the ambition
At least two linking words are used
Sentences vary in length (not all short)
The paragraph ends with a forward-looking closing sentence

title: Formal Letters
blurb: Write proper formal letters: the format, the introduction formula, body and conclusion.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Recognise when a letter must be formal.
  • Lay out every part of a formal letter correctly.
  • Write a strong introduction using a clear four-step formula.
  • Develop the body, then write a proper conclusion and signature.
GoalBy the end, you could write a real letter to your assemblyman, an editor, or an employer.
slides.say: Welcome to formal letters. Here are our objectives. By the end of this module, you will be able to recognise when a letter must be formal. You will lay out every part of a formal letter correctly. You will write a strong introduction using a clear four step formula. And you will develop the body, then write a proper conclusion and signature. By the end, you could write a real letter to your assemblyman, to an editor, or to an employer.
slides.example: A formal letter has a sender's address, date, recipient's address, salutation, heading, body, and signature.

slides.html:
A formal letter is written to someone in an official position — a president, minister, headmaster, assemblyman, an editor, or an employer.

How to identify oneLook at who you are writing to. If they hold an office or position, the letter must be formal.

Formal letters use formal language only — no slang, no jargon, no chatty expressions.
slides.say: A formal letter is written to someone in an official position. For example, a president, a minister, a headmaster, an assemblyman, an editor, or an employer. Here is how to identify one. Look at who you are writing to. If they hold an office or a position, then the letter must be formal. Formal letters use formal language only. No slang, no jargon, and no chatty expressions.
slides.example: A formal letter is written to someone in an official position, in polite, formal language.

slides.h: The parts of a formal letter
slides.html:
Every formal letter has these parts, in order:

  • Sender's address + date (top right)
  • Recipient's address (left)
  • Salutation — Dear Sir/Madam
  • Heading — the topic, in capitals
  • Body — introduction, main body, conclusion
  • Subscription — Yours faithfully,
  • Signature and full name
slides.say: Every formal letter has these parts, in order. The sender's address and the date, at the top right. The recipient's address, on the left. The salutation, such as Dear Sir or Dear Madam. The heading, which is the topic, written in capitals. The body, made of an introduction, a main body, and a conclusion. The subscription, which is, yours faithfully. And finally, your signature and full name.
slides.example: A formal letter runs from the sender's address and date to the salutation, heading, body, and signature.

slides.html:
Write your institution's address (not your personal name) at the top right. It can be:

  • Blocked — each line starts at the same point
  • Slanted — each line steps slightly to the right

It may be punctuated or unpunctuated — just be consistent.

BlockedDome D/A JHS
P. O. Box 5
Dome-Accra
RuleUse the institution's address, never the writer's name.
slides.say: Write your institution's address, not your personal name, at the top right. It can be blocked, where each line starts at the same point. Or slanted, where each line steps slightly to the right. It may be punctuated or unpunctuated, but be consistent. For example, a blocked address reads: Dome D A J H S, P O Box 5, Dome Accra. Remember the rule. Use the institution's address, never the writer's name.
slides.example: Dome D/A JHS, P. O. Box 5, Dome-Accra.

slides.html:
The date comes directly under the sender's address, on the right, with no blank line between them.

Write it in numbers or in words — never mix the two.

Wrong5/May/2005  ·  25-may-2005
Correct25th May, 2005  ·  25-05-2005
slides.say: The date comes directly under the sender's address, on the right, with no blank line between them. Write it in numbers, or in words, but never mix the two. For example, five slash May slash 2005 is wrong, because it mixes numbers and words. Correct versions are: the twenty fifth of May, 2005, in words. Or, 25 dash 05 dash 2005, in numbers.

slides.html:
The recipient's address goes on the left, below the sender's address. Use their title and institution, not their personal name.

The Assemblyman,
P. O. Box 5,
Dome-Accra.

Then the salutation: Dear Sir (man), Dear Madam (woman), or Dear Sir/Madam if unsure.
slides.say: The recipient's address goes on the left, below the sender's address. Use their title and institution, not their personal name. For example: The Assemblyman, P O Box 5, Dome Accra. Then comes the salutation. Use Dear Sir for a man, Dear Madam for a woman, or Dear Sir slash Madam if you are not sure.
slides.example: The Assemblyman, P. O. Box 5, Dome-Accra. Dear Sir,

slides.html:
The heading states what the letter is about. It comes from the question you were given.

Write it in capital letters (or capitalised) and underline it.

HeadingTHE PROBLEMS FACING MY SCHOOL
slides.say: The heading states what the letter is about, and it comes from the question you were given. Write it in capital letters, or capitalised, and underline it. For example: THE PROBLEMS FACING MY SCHOOL.
slides.example: THE PROBLEMS FACING MY SCHOOL

slides.html:
Keep the introduction short. Follow four steps:

  • 1. Formal expression to introduce the topic: I wish to draw your attention to [topic].
  • 2. Background: There are several [topic].
  • 3. Sub-points: The [topic] are: …; …; and … .
  • 4. Readiness: Below are the details of [topic].
Other openersI wish to inform you that…; I would like to bring to your notice…
slides.say: Keep the introduction short, and follow four steps. Step one, a formal expression to introduce the topic. For example: I wish to draw your attention to the topic. Step two, the background. For example: There are several of the topic. Step three, the sub-points. State, the topics are, and then list them. Step four, show readiness. Say: Below are the details of the topic. Other useful openers include: I wish to inform you that. Or: I would like to bring to your notice.
slides.example: I wish to draw your attention to the problems facing my school. There are several of these problems. They are inadequate furniture, a lack of textbooks, and a leaking roof. The details are below.

slides.html:
Topic: The causes of teenage pregnancy in the country.

I wish to draw your attention to the causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. There are several causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. The causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Below are the details of the causes of teenage pregnancy in the country.
NoticeAll four steps appear, and each names the topic clearly.
slides.say: Here is a worked example, on the topic, the causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. I wish to draw your attention to the causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. There are several causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. The causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Below are the details of the causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. Notice that all four steps appear, and each one names the topic clearly.
slides.example: I wish to draw your attention to the causes of teenage pregnancy in this town. There are several causes. They are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. The details are below.

slides.html:
Each body paragraph develops one point, in this order:

  • Topic sentence with a transition: The first cause is…
  • Supporting details — explain, and give an example
  • Result and effect of the point
  • Solution — a polite appeal: Please, sir, I would appeal that…

Use transitions: firstly, in addition, again, finally. Keep it formal — no slang.
slides.say: Each body paragraph develops one point, in this order. First, a topic sentence with a transition, such as: the first cause is. Then supporting details, where you explain the point and give an example. Then the result and the effect of the point. Then a solution, which is a polite appeal, such as: Please, sir, I would appeal that. Use transitions like firstly, in addition, again, and finally. And keep it formal, with no slang.
slides.example: The first problem facing my school is inadequate furniture. Many pupils sit on the bare floor during lessons. This makes writing difficult and lowers their concentration. I therefore appeal for desks to be supplied.

slides.html:
Sir, the first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. Many parents fail to provide food, shelter and moral guidance because they are too busy with work. For instance, some children sleep on the streets and search for their needs elsewhere. As a result, they fall into harmful relationships, which leads to teenage pregnancy. Please, sir, I would appeal that parents be educated to care properly for their children.

One point, fully developed, ending with a clear appeal.
slides.say: Here is a model body paragraph. Sir, the first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. Many parents fail to provide food, shelter and moral guidance, because they are too busy with work. For instance, some children sleep on the streets and search for their needs elsewhere. As a result, they fall into harmful relationships, which leads to teenage pregnancy. Please sir, I would appeal that parents be educated to care properly for their children. Notice: one point, fully developed, ending with a clear appeal.
slides.example: Sir, the first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. Many parents fail to provide their children's basic needs. As a result, some girls seek money through premarital sex, which leads to pregnancy. Parents should therefore care properly for their children.

slides.html:
End the letter in two moves:

  • Summarise your points.
  • Give a definite stand or hope.

Use transitions: to conclude, in conclusion, in a nutshell.

In a nutshell, the causes of teenage pregnancy are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. I hope the remedies suggested will be taken into consideration.
slides.say: End the letter in two moves. First, summarise your points. Then give a definite stand, or a hope. Use transitions like, to conclude, in conclusion, or in a nutshell. For example: In a nutshell, the causes of teenage pregnancy are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. I hope the remedies suggested will be taken into consideration.
slides.example: In a nutshell, the problems facing my school are inadequate furniture, a lack of textbooks, and a leaking roof. I hope you will give them your urgent attention.

slides.html:
Close a formal letter with Yours faithfully, (with the comma). Then sign, and write your full name.

Yours faithfully,

(Daniel Abankaka)
NoteBecause you usually do not know the official personally, the correct close is Yours faithfully.
slides.say: Close a formal letter with, yours faithfully, including the comma. Then sign your signature, and write your full name. For example: Yours faithfully, then on a new line, in brackets, Daniel Abankaka. A note: because you usually do not know the official personally, the correct close is, yours faithfully.
slides.example: Yours faithfully, (signature) Daniel Abankaka.

slides.html:
The same format fits several letters; only the opening changes:

  • Official letter: I wish to draw your attention to the problems facing my school.
  • Letter to the editor: Permit me space in your esteemed newspaper to bring to the public's attention…
  • Application letter: I wish to apply for employment as a salesboy in your establishment. I am a boy of seventeen who has completed junior high school…
slides.say: The same format fits several kinds of letter. Only the opening changes. For an official letter, you might begin: I wish to draw your attention to the problems facing my school. For a letter to the editor, begin: Permit me space in your esteemed newspaper to bring to the public's attention. And for an application letter, begin: I wish to apply for employment as a salesboy in your establishment. I am a boy of seventeen who has completed junior high school.
slides.example: I am writing to apply for the post of office assistant advertised in the Daily Graphic of 5th May.

slides.html:
Let us recap the formal letter:

  • Format: sender's address + date (right), recipient (left), salutation, heading, body, subscription, signature.
  • Introduction formula: formal expression → background → sub-points → readiness.
  • Body: one expository point per paragraph, ending in a polite appeal.
  • Conclusion: summarise + definite stand, then Yours faithfully,
Well doneYou now have a complete formula for any formal letter. Practise it on the steps above!
slides.say: Let us recap the formal letter. The format is: sender's address and date on the right, the recipient's address on the left, then the salutation, the heading, the body, the subscription, and your signature. The introduction formula is: formal expression, then background, then sub-points, then readiness. The body has one expository point per paragraph, ending in a polite appeal. And the conclusion summarises and gives a definite stand, before, yours faithfully. Well done. You now have a complete formula for any formal letter.
slides.example: A formal letter keeps a polite tone and follows its fixed format from address to signature.

rewrite.weak: Hi sir, I just wanna tell u about some problems in our school. There are problems. Thanks a lot!
rewrite.hint: Rewrite this as a proper formal introduction using the four-step formula. No slang.
rewrite.model: I wish to draw your attention to the problems facing my school. There are several problems facing my school. These problems are inadequate teaching staff, inadequate classrooms, and a lack of toilets. Below are the details of the problems facing my school.
rubric: No slang or chatty expressions remain
Uses a formal opener (I wish to draw your attention to...)
States 'there are several...', then lists the sub-points
Ends with the readiness line (Below are the details...)

points.passage: Sir, the first problem facing my school is inadequate teaching staff. The school has only three teachers for all the subjects. As a result, the teachers cannot complete the syllabus before the BECE. The effect is that most candidates fail every year. Please, sir, I would appeal that the school be provided with adequate teaching staff.
model: The point: the school has inadequate teaching staff
Only three teachers cover all subjects
Result: the syllabus is not completed before the BECE
Effect: most candidates fail each year
It ends with a polite appeal for more teachers

points: Topic: the problems facing my school
frompoints.instruction: Write the INTRODUCTION of a formal letter to your assemblyman using the four-step formula.
frompoints.model: I wish to draw your attention to the problems facing my school. There are several problems facing my school. These problems are inadequate teaching staff, inadequate classrooms, and a lack of toilets. Below are the details of the problems facing my school.
rubric: Step 1: a formal opener naming the topic
Step 3: the three sub-points are listed

question.q: Write the introduction AND one body paragraph of a formal letter to the head of WAEC on a cause of examination malpractice (e.g. inadequate preparation).
question.model:
I wish to draw your attention to the causes of examination malpractice in Ghana. There are several causes of examination malpractice in the country. The causes are inadequate preparation, societal pressure, and weak enforcement. Below are the details of the causes of examination malpractice in the country.

Sir, firstly, inadequate preparation is a major cause. Many students do not study enough, often due to poor study habits or a lack of learning resources. As a result, they resort to cheating to pass. In effect, they grow used to dishonesty. Please, sir, I would appeal that students be guided to prepare well in advance of their examinations.
rubric: Body opens with a transition and topic sentence
It gives a supporting detail and an example
It states a result/effect and ends with a polite appeal

title: Article Writing
blurb: Write articles for newspapers and magazines: title, the two introduction methods, opinionated body, and conclusion.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Lay out an article's features (title, introduction, main body, conclusion, name).
  • Write an introduction by the definition and general impression methods.
  • Develop the body with opinion language and conclude well.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will lay out an article's features. You will write an introduction by the definition method, and the general impression method. And you will develop the body with opinion language, and conclude well.
slides.example: An article informs the public on an important issue, with a title, introduction, body, and conclusion.

slides.html:
An article is an essay written to inform the public on an important issue of societal interest. Articles appear in newspapers, magazines, posters and social media.

FeaturesTitle · Introduction · Main body · Conclusion · Name & address of the writer.
slides.say: An article is an essay written to inform the public on an important issue of societal interest. Articles appear in newspapers, magazines, posters, and social media. Its features are: a title, an introduction, the main body, a conclusion, and the name and address of the writer.
slides.example: An article is an essay written to inform the public on an important issue of societal interest.

slides.html:
The title comes from the question. Write it in capital letters, neatly underlined.

THE CAUSES OF TEENAGE PREGNANCY IN THE COUNTRY
slides.say: The title comes from the question. Write it in capital letters, neatly underlined. For example: THE CAUSES OF TEENAGE PREGNANCY IN THE COUNTRY.
slides.example: THE CAUSES OF TEENAGE PREGNANCY IN THE COUNTRY

slides.html:
Steps: define the topic → highlight the public's woes → bring out sub-points → show readiness.

According to the Oxford dictionary, teenage pregnancy occurs between the ages of thirteen and nineteen. Recently, the numbers have increased. Some of the causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Below are the details of the causes of teenage pregnancy in the country.
slides.say: The definition method has four steps: define the topic, highlight the public's woes, bring out the sub-points, then show readiness. For example: According to the Oxford dictionary, teenage pregnancy occurs between the ages of thirteen and nineteen. Recently, the numbers have increased. Some of the causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Below are the details of the causes of teenage pregnancy in the country.
slides.example: According to the dictionary, teenage pregnancy occurs between thirteen and nineteen. Recently, the numbers have risen sharply. The causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Below are the details.

slides.html:
Steps: show how the public laments the topic → show your concern to join the talk → bring out sub-points → show readiness.

A recent increase in teenage pregnancies has generated public debate in the media. As a citizen, I would like to express my view on the causes. There are several causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. The causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Below are the details.
TipThis method is safest — the definition method fails if you define the topic wrongly.
slides.say: The general impression method has four steps: show how the public laments the topic, show your concern to join the talk, bring out the sub-points, then show readiness. For example: A recent increase in teenage pregnancies has generated public debate in the media. As a citizen, I would like to express my view on the causes. There are several causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. The causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Below are the details. A tip: this method is the safest, because the definition method fails if you define the topic wrongly.
slides.example: The recent rise in teenage pregnancies has caused much public debate. As a citizen, I wish to share my view on the causes. The causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Below are the details.

slides.h: The main body — use opinion language
slides.html:
The body presents your points in expository paragraphs, but with opinion expressions:

  • In my opinion · In my view · I believe · I think · I suggest

Open a paragraph or offer a suggestion with these. Keep it formal — no slang.
slides.say: The body presents your points in expository paragraphs, but with opinion expressions. For example: in my opinion, in my view, I believe, I think, or I suggest. Use these to open a paragraph, or to offer a suggestion. Keep it formal, with no slang.
slides.example: In my opinion, the first cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care, as many parents fail to provide their children's needs.

slides.html: I believe a major cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. Many parents do not provide food, shelter or moral training, because they are busy with work. For instance, some children sleep on the streets at Kejetia. As a result, the girls seek money through premarital sex, leading to pregnancy. I think parents should be supported to care for their children.
slides.say: Here is a model body paragraph. I believe a major cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. Many parents do not provide food, shelter, or moral training, because they are busy with work. For instance, some children sleep on the streets at Kejetia. As a result, the girls seek money through premarital sex, leading to pregnancy. I think parents should be supported to care for their children.
slides.example: I believe a major cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. Many parents do not provide food, shelter or moral guidance because they are busy working. For instance, some children sleep on the streets at Kejetia. As a result, some girls turn to premarital sex, leading to pregnancy. I think parents should be supported to care for their children.

slides.html:
Conclude like a formal letter, but not conversational: summarise and give a final hope or call to action. End with your name.

In a nutshell, the causes of teenage pregnancy are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. I hope more vigilance will be taken to curb this social problem.
slides.say: Conclude like a formal letter, but not conversational. Summarise, and give a final hope, or a call to action. Then end with your name. For example: In a nutshell, the causes of teenage pregnancy are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. I hope more vigilance will be taken to curb this social problem.
slides.example: In a nutshell, the causes of teenage pregnancy are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. I hope greater vigilance will be taken to curb this problem. — Atagaga Siagaga.

slides.html:
For blogs and magazines aimed at general readers, articles can be informal: a conversational tone, simple language, personal stories, humour, rhetorical questions and contractions.

Ever notice how the school canteen is the heart of social life on campus? It's where we all gather to grab a bite, catch up with friends and share laughs.
slides.say: For blogs and magazines aimed at general readers, articles can be informal. They use a conversational tone, simple language, personal stories, humour, rhetorical questions, and contractions. For example: Ever notice how the school canteen is the heart of social life on campus? It's where we all gather to grab a bite, catch up with friends, and share laughs.
slides.example: Ever notice how the school canteen is the heart of campus life? It's where we grab a bite, catch up with friends, and share plenty of laughs.

slides.html: A rejoinder is a formal response to a published article, correcting inaccuracies. It uses letter format: sender's address, date, Reference (Re:) the article, recipient's address, salutation, body (introduce → correct the points with evidence → conclude), and Yours faithfully.
slides.say: A rejoinder is a formal response to a published article, correcting its inaccuracies. It uses the letter format: your address, the date, a Reference line naming the article, the recipient's address, the salutation, the body, which introduces your purpose, corrects the points with evidence, and concludes, and finally, yours faithfully.
slides.example: I wish to respond to the article 'Galamsey is Harmless' published on 5th May, which contains several inaccuracies I would like to correct.

slides.h: A complete article — study every part
slides.html:
THE CAUSES OF TEENAGE PREGNANCY IN THE COUNTRY

The recent increase in the number of teenage pregnancies in the country has generated a lot of public debate in the media. As a citizen, I would like to express my view on the causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. There are several causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. The causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Below are the details of the causes of teenage pregnancy in the country.

I believe a major cause of teenage pregnancy is inadequate parental care. Many parents do not take good care of their children. Most parents in the country do not provide their children with food, accommodation and clothing. In addition, parents do not give their children moral training, because they are always busy with work due to the economic hardships in the country. As a result, most children fend for themselves, and some teenage girls beg or extort money from men for sex. The effect is that the girls may get pregnant through premarital sex, sometimes leading to untimely death through illegal abortion. I think the government should encourage parents to take adequate care of their children.

In my opinion, poverty is another cause of teenage pregnancy. Poverty levels among families in the country are high, and many families cannot provide their children's basic needs. This can be explained by the fact that the economic hardships do not allow parents to find gainful employment. As a result, some parents push their girl-child into early relationships to support the family. For instance, according to the GES annual report, about 10,000 JHS girls dropped out of school last year due to teenage pregnancy. If the rate is not reduced through measures such as the school feeding programme, I am afraid the figures will keep rising. I suggest the government does all it can to reduce poverty.

Finally, I think peer influence causes teenage pregnancy. Young people are easily influenced by their friends, who may lure them into premarital sex in exchange for items such as phones. The result is teenage pregnancy, and the effect is that many of these girls become single parents for life. Young people should be mindful of the company they keep, and I hope parents will help their children choose good friends.

To crown it all, teenage pregnancy, as one of the social problems in the country, can be controlled now that we have identified the causes. I hope the whole country will take bold steps to tackle it.

— Atagaga Siagaga, Dome D/A JHS.
slides.say: Here is a complete worked article on the causes of teenage pregnancy. Listen for the parts you have learned: the introduction, with the public's woes, the writer's concern, the points, and the readiness. Then each body paragraph opens with opinion language, like I believe, in my opinion, and I think, develops one cause with details, an example, a result and an effect, and offers a suggestion. Notice the statistic from the G E S report in the second body paragraph. Finally, the conclusion summarises and ends with a hope, followed by the writer's name and school. Read it slowly and see how every part you studied appears in a real article.

slides.html:
Recap: an article informs the public. Title in capitals; introduction by the definition or general impression method; body in expository paragraphs with opinion language; conclusion that summarises with a final hope; then your name. Articles can be formal or informal, and a rejoinder replies to a published piece.

Well doneYou can now write an article for any newspaper or magazine.
slides.say: Recap. An article informs the public. The title is in capitals. The introduction uses the definition method, or the general impression method. The body is in expository paragraphs, with opinion language. The conclusion summarises, with a final hope, then your name. Articles can be formal or informal, and a rejoinder replies to a published piece. Well done. You can now write an article for any newspaper or magazine.
slides.example: An article informs the public through a clear title, a strong introduction, an opinionated body, and a hopeful conclusion.
