rewrite.hint: Rewrite as a proper article introduction using the general impression method (woes → concern → points → readiness). No slang.
rewrite.model: The recent increase in teenage pregnancies has generated much public debate in the media. As a concerned citizen, I would like to express my view on the causes. There are several causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. The causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Below are the details of the causes of teenage pregnancy in the country.
rubric: Shows concern to join the discussion
Lists the sub-points as noun phrases

points.passage: The recent increase in the number of teenage pregnancies in the country has generated a lot of public debate in the media. As a citizen, I would like to express my view on the causes of teenage pregnancy. There are several causes of teenage pregnancy in the country. The causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. Below are the details of the causes of teenage pregnancy in the country.
model: Method: general impression method
The woes: the rise in teenage pregnancy has caused public debate
Concern: 'As a citizen, I would like to express my view'
The points: inadequate parental care; poverty; peer influence
Readiness: 'Below are the details...'

frompoints.instruction: Write the INTRODUCTION of an article using the general impression method.
frompoints.model: In recent times, the falling reading habits of young people have become a topic of concern in the media. As a citizen, I wish to share my view on the importance of reading. There are several reasons reading is important. The reasons are the provision of knowledge, the improvement of language, and the relaxation of the mind. Below are the details of the importance of reading.
rubric: Shows intent to join the discussion

question.q: Write a full article (introduction + ONE body paragraph + conclusion) for a newspaper on the measures to control road accidents. Use opinion language.
question.model:
MEASURES TO CONTROL ROAD ACCIDENTS IN THE COUNTRY

The rising number of road accidents has become a source of great worry in the media. As a concerned citizen, I would like to share my view on the measures to control them. There are several measures to control road accidents. The measures are stricter law enforcement, public education, and better roads. Below are the details of the measures.

In my opinion, the first measure is stricter law enforcement. The police must punish over-speeding and drunk driving firmly. For instance, regular checkpoints and speed cameras can catch offenders. As a result, drivers would become more careful, and accidents would fall. I suggest that the authorities resource the police to enforce the traffic laws.

In a nutshell, road accidents can be reduced through stricter enforcement, public education, and better roads. I hope the authorities will act quickly to save lives on our roads.
rubric: Title in capitals; clear introduction (woes → concern → points → readiness)
Body paragraph uses opinion language (I believe/in my opinion)
Body develops one point with example/result
Conclusion summarises with a final hope

title: Writing & Delivering News
blurb: Report the news: headline, lead, body (inverted pyramid) and conclusion — plus delivery tips.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Structure a news report: headline, lead, body, conclusion.
  • Open a lead with the 5 Ws and H.
  • Keep a clear, concise, objective style and deliver it well.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will structure a news report with a headline, a lead, a body, and a conclusion. You will open a lead with the five W's and H. And you will keep a clear, concise, objective style, and deliver it well.
slides.example: A news report has a headline, a lead answering the 5 Ws and H, a body, and a conclusion.

slides.html: A news presentation informs the audience about current events clearly, concisely and engagingly. Use credible sources, double-check facts, and present a balanced view.
slides.say: A news presentation informs the audience about current events, clearly, concisely, and engagingly. Use credible sources, double check your facts, and present a balanced view.
slides.example: A good news report informs the audience about current events clearly, concisely, and accurately.

slides.html:
The headline is catchy and concise, summarising the story.

The lead (introduction) hooks the audience and answers the key questions: who, what, when, where, why and how.

Rising Tides: Coastal Cities Under Threat from Climate Change.
slides.say: The headline is catchy and concise, summarising the story. The lead, or introduction, hooks the audience, and answers the key questions: who, what, when, where, why, and how. For example, a headline: Rising Tides: Coastal Cities Under Threat from Climate Change.
slides.example: Rising Tides: Coastal Communities Face New Threat. Heavy flooding struck three coastal towns yesterday, displacing hundreds of families.

slides.html: The body gives details, context, quotes and statistics. It follows the inverted pyramid: the most important facts come first, then supporting detail.
slides.say: The body gives the details, context, quotes, and statistics. It follows the inverted pyramid: the most important facts come first, then the supporting detail.
slides.example: The flooding began on Tuesday night after six hours of heavy rain. Officials say more than 300 homes were affected, and relief teams have since moved in.

slides.html:
Use short, relevant quotes from credible sources to support the story.

As Dr. Smith warned, 'The damage could soon be beyond repair if we don't act now.'
slides.say: Use short, relevant quotes from credible sources to support your story. For example: As Doctor Smith warned, the damage could soon be beyond repair if we don't act now.
slides.example: As the district officer warned, 'Residents in low-lying areas must move to higher ground immediately.'

slides.html:
Keep it clear (simple words), concise (every word counts) and neutral (no personal opinion).

The conclusion recaps the key points and may add a call to action or look ahead.
slides.say: Keep it clear, using simple words. Keep it concise, because every word counts. And keep it neutral, with no personal opinion. The conclusion recaps the key points, and may add a call to action, or a look ahead.
slides.example: The authorities have promised lasting drainage repairs, and residents now wait to see whether the long-standing problem will finally be solved.

slides.html: When presenting aloud: practise, watch your timing, and use good voice modulation, eye contact, body language and pauses. Thank the audience at the end.
slides.say: When presenting aloud: practise, watch your timing, and use good voice modulation, eye contact, body language, and pauses. And thank the audience at the end.
slides.example: Good evening. Tonight, our top story examines the flooding that has displaced hundreds of families along the coast.

slides.html:
Recap: news = headline → lead (5 Ws & H) → body (inverted pyramid) → conclusion. Style is clear, concise and objective; quotes are short and credible; delivery is practised and confident.

Well doneYou can now write and present a news story.
slides.say: Recap. A news story has a headline, then a lead answering the five W's and H, then a body in the inverted pyramid, then a conclusion. The style is clear, concise, and objective. Quotes are short and credible. And the delivery is practised and confident. Well done. You can now write and present a news story.
slides.example: A news report moves from a catchy headline to a clear lead, a detailed body, and a brief conclusion.

rewrite.weak: there was an accident on the road yesterday and some people were hurt it was very sad and scary for everyone who saw it
rewrite.hint: Rewrite as a clear, objective news lead answering who/what/when/where. Remove emotional/opinion words.
rewrite.model: A serious road crash on the Accra–Kumasi highway yesterday left several people injured, police have confirmed. The accident, involving two vehicles, occurred near Nsawam at about 5 p.m. during heavy traffic.
rubric: Reads as an objective news lead (no emotion words)

points.passage: Good evening. Coastal cities around the world are facing an unprecedented threat from climate change. Rising sea levels and stronger storms are putting millions of lives and billions of dollars at risk. Tonight, we explore how climate change is affecting these regions and what is being done about it.
model: This is the lead/introduction of a news report
What: coastal cities threatened by climate change
Cause: rising sea levels and stronger storms
Stakes: millions of lives and billions of dollars at risk
It hooks the audience and previews the story

points: What: illegal mining contaminating water bodies
Effect: threatens drinking water and farming
frompoints.instruction: Write a news HEADLINE and LEAD from these points (objective tone, answer who/what/where).
frompoints.model:
Headline: "Galamsey: Ghana's Rivers Under Threat from Illegal Mining"

Lead: Illegal mining, known locally as galamsey, is severely polluting major rivers across Ghana, the Ministry of Environment has warned. The contamination threatens drinking water and farming for thousands of communities that depend on these rivers.

question.q: Write a short news report (headline, lead, one body paragraph, conclusion) about a school winning a regional science competition.
question.model:
Headline: "Top Honours: Local School Wins Regional Science Prize"

Lead: Students of Unity Senior High School have won this year's regional science and technology competition, organised in Kumasi last Saturday. The team beat twelve other schools to claim the top award.

Body: The competition tested students in physics, robotics and problem-solving. According to the organisers, Unity's winning project was a low-cost water filter built from local materials. "Their work showed real creativity," said the chief judge, Mr. Owusu.

Conclusion: The school will now represent the region at the national finals next month. Teachers and parents have praised the students for their hard work and have called for more support for science education.
rubric: Body adds detail and a short quote

title: Advertisement Creation
blurb: Persuade an audience to act: headline, hook, benefits over features, USP, emotional appeal, social proof and a call to action.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Set a clear objective and know your target audience.
  • Write a catchy headline + hook, sell benefits over features, and state a USP.
  • Use emotional appeal, social proof and a strong call to action.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will set a clear objective, and know your target audience. You will write a catchy headline and hook, sell benefits over features, and state a unique selling point. And you will use emotional appeal, social proof, and a strong call to action.
slides.example: A persuasive advert uses a headline, a hook, benefits, a unique selling point, and a call to action.

slides.html: A persuasive advertisement convinces an audience to take an action — buy a product, use a service, or support a cause. It blends creative and persuasive techniques.
slides.say: A persuasive advertisement convinces an audience to take an action: to buy a product, use a service, or support a cause. It blends creative and persuasive techniques.
slides.example: A persuasive advertisement convinces the audience to buy a product, use a service, or support a cause.

slides.html: Define the objective (what action do you want?) and the target audience (age, interests, needs). Tailor the message to them.
slides.say: First, define the objective: what action do you want the reader to take? Then define the target audience: their age, interests, and needs. And tailor the message to them.
slides.example: This advert aims to persuade busy students to buy an affordable study lamp that protects their eyes.

slides.html:
The headline grabs attention and states the main benefit. The hook opens with a question or pain point.

Transform Your Body in Just 30 Days! Do you struggle to find time for the gym?
slides.say: The headline grabs attention, and states the main benefit. The hook opens with a question, or a pain point. For example: Transform your body in just thirty days! Do you struggle to find time for the gym?
slides.example: STUDY BRIGHTER, SEE CLEARER! Tired of straining your eyes over late-night homework?

slides.html:
Stress the benefits (how it improves the reader's life), not just features. State the USP — what makes it unique.

Achieve your goals faster with personalised plans. Unlike other apps, we give real-time feedback from certified trainers.
slides.say: Stress the benefits, that is, how it improves the reader's life, not just the features. And state the unique selling point: what makes it different. For example: Achieve your goals faster with personalised plans. Unlike other apps, we give real-time feedback from certified trainers.
slides.example: The ClearGlow lamp floods your desk with soft, even light that protects your eyes. Unlike ordinary lamps, it lasts three times longer.

slides.html:
Add an emotional appeal, then social proof (testimonials, ratings), and end with a strong call to action and a sense of urgency.

"I lost 20 pounds in 3 months!" — Jane D. Download now and start your 7-day free trial. Limited time offer!
slides.say: Add an emotional appeal, then social proof, such as testimonials or ratings, and end with a strong call to action, and a sense of urgency. For example: I lost twenty pounds in three months, says Jane D. Download now and start your seven day free trial. Limited time offer!
slides.example: Imagine acing every exam in perfect light. 'Best lamp I've ever owned,' says Ama D. Order now and enjoy a 20% launch discount, this week only!

slides.html:
Recap an advert: objective → audience → headline + hook → benefits over features → USP → emotional appeal → social proof → call to action (with urgency). Keep all parts consistent.

Well doneYou can now write an advert that persuades.
slides.say: Recap an advertisement. Set the objective, know the audience, write a headline and hook, sell benefits over features, state your unique selling point, add an emotional appeal, give social proof, and end with a call to action, with urgency. Keep all the parts consistent. Well done. You can now write an advert that persuades.
slides.example: A strong advert pairs a catchy headline and real benefits with social proof and a clear call to action.

title: Speech Writing
blurb: Write a speech to deliver before an audience: heading, address of the house, body with audience contact, and conclusion.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Write a speech heading (speaker, date, occasion, time, place).
  • Open with the six introduction steps (address the house → readiness).
  • Keep audience contact in the body and close properly.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will write a speech heading. You will open with the six introduction steps, from addressing the house to showing readiness. And you will keep audience contact in the body, and close properly.
slides.example: A speech has a full heading, a six-step introduction, a body with audience contact, and a closing.

slides.html: A speech is written to be delivered before an audience. It must have audience contact — you involve the listeners while delivering. Examples: a nation address, a sermon.
slides.say: A speech is written to be delivered before an audience. It must have audience contact, meaning you involve the listeners while you deliver it. Examples are a national address, or a sermon.
slides.example: A speech is a piece written to be delivered before an audience, keeping the listeners involved.

slides.html:
The heading states the speaker, the topic, the time, the date, the occasion and the venue.

A SPEECH DELIVERED BY KOFI AMPA ON THE CAUSES OF TEENAGE PREGNANCY AT 2:00 PM ON 25TH MAY 2015 DURING THE SPEECH AND PRIZE-GIVING DAY AT THE SCHOOL PARK, AMENASE.
slides.say: The heading states the speaker, the topic, the time, the date, the occasion, and the venue. For example: A speech delivered by Kofi Ampa on the causes of teenage pregnancy, at two p m, on the twenty fifth of May 2015, during the speech and prize giving day, at the school park, Amenase.
slides.example: A SPEECH DELIVERED BY KOFI AMPA ON THE CAUSES OF TEENAGE PREGNANCY DURING THE SPEECH AND PRIZE-GIVING DAY AT THE SCHOOL PARK, AMENASE.

slides.h: Six steps to open a speech
slides.html:
• Address the house (the dignitaries).
  • Show respect for the chance to speak.
  • Welcome the guests.
  • State the purpose of the occasion.
  • Bring out your sub-points.
  • Show readiness.
slides.say: There are six steps to open a speech. Address the house, that is, the dignitaries. Show respect for the chance to speak. Welcome the guests. State the purpose of the occasion. Bring out your sub-points. And show readiness.
slides.example: Mr. Chairman, the Headmaster, Invited Guests, Parents, Ladies and Gentlemen. I consider it a great honour to speak this morning on the causes of teenage pregnancy.

slides.html: Mr. Chairman, the Headmaster, Invited Guests, Parents, Chiefs, Ladies and Gentlemen. I believe it is a great honour to deliver a speech this morning on the causes of teenage pregnancy. May I welcome you all to this grand occasion — Akwaaba! This is the time for past and present students to deliberate on important topics such as teenage pregnancy. These causes are inadequate parental care, poverty and peer influence. My speech will show the details.
slides.say: Here is a model introduction. Mr. Chairman, the Headmaster, Invited Guests, Parents, Chiefs, Ladies and Gentlemen. I believe it is a great honour to deliver a speech this morning on the causes of teenage pregnancy. May I welcome you all to this grand occasion. Akwaaba! This is the time for past and present students to deliberate on important topics, such as teenage pregnancy. These causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. My speech will show the details.
slides.example: Mr. Chairman, the Headmaster, Invited Guests, Parents, Ladies and Gentlemen. It is a great honour to deliver a speech this morning on the causes of teenage pregnancy. May I welcome you all. The causes are inadequate parental care, poverty, and peer influence. My speech will show the details.

slides.html:
Present points in well-organised expository paragraphs, but keep audience contact with rhetorical questions (questions that need no answer) and address the chairman.

Mr. Chairman, the first cause is inadequate parental care. Am I not telling the truth? Tell me!
slides.say: Present your points in well organised expository paragraphs, but keep audience contact, using rhetorical questions, which are questions that need no answer, and by addressing the chairman. For example: Mr. Chairman, the first cause is inadequate parental care. Am I not telling the truth? Tell me!
slides.example: Mr. Chairman, the first cause is inadequate parental care. Am I not telling the truth? Tell me! Many parents fail to provide their children's basic needs.

slides.html:
Close in three moves: address the house → give your conclusion → thank the guests.

Mr. Chairman, ... Ladies and Gentlemen, to crown it all, if we rally behind the headmaster, the school will bounce back. Thank you.
slides.say: Close in three moves. Address the house. Give your conclusion. Then thank the guests. For example: Mr. Chairman, Ladies and Gentlemen, to crown it all, if we rally behind the headmaster, the school will bounce back. Thank you.
slides.example: Mr. Chairman, Ladies and Gentlemen, to crown it all, if we all play our part, teenage pregnancy in our school will fall. Thank you.

slides.h: A complete speech — study every part
slides.html:
A SPEECH DELIVERED BY KOFI AMPA ON THE CAUSES OF TEENAGE PREGNANCY AT 2:00 PM ON 25TH MAY 2015 DURING THE SPEECH AND PRIZE-GIVING DAY AT THE SCHOOL PARK, AMENASE

Mr. Chairman, The Minister of Education, The Headmaster, Staff, Chiefs, Invited Guests, Fellow Students, Ladies and Gentlemen. I consider it a great honour to be called upon this afternoon to deliver a speech on the causes of teenage pregnancy in our school during this grand occasion. May I use this opportunity to welcome you all. Welcome! This occasion is the time for both past and present students to deliberate on pressing issues such as the causes of teenage pregnancy. There are several causes of teenage pregnancy in the school. The causes are inadequate parental care, broken homes, and poverty. My speech will detail the causes.

Mr. Chairman, the first cause of teenage pregnancy in our dear school is inadequate parental care. The parents in the community do not take good care of their children. Am I not telling the truth? Tell me. Some parents do not provide their children with food, clothing, and, most shocking of all, a place to sleep. What should these children do? Tell me! Their only choice is to leave home to search for their basic needs, and as a result some use premarital sex for money. The effect is that the teenage girls get pregnant, sometimes leading to death through illegal abortion. Parents must therefore take good care of their children, and I appeal to the authorities to put measures in place to help control this.

Mr. Chairman, another cause is poverty. As the saying goes, poverty is madness. Poverty makes parents powerless to look after their children. Desperate to escape it, some parents push their teenage girls to associate with men for money. That money may relieve the parents' stress, but it leaves behind teenage pregnancy and effects that cannot be erased. As we have government officials here, it is their duty to help improve the economy and reduce poverty.

Mr. Chairman, the final cause is broken homes. Families separate nowadays, and single parents struggle to care for their children. As a result, the children cannot decide which parent to turn to for their needs, and some enter premarital sex for money due to neglect. Everyone here must do all they can to keep families together.

Mr. Chairman, The Minister of Education, The Headmaster, Staff, Chiefs, Invited Guests, Fellow Students, Ladies and Gentlemen. From the above speech, we hope to do our part to protect our children and reduce teenage pregnancy in the school and the community.

Thank you.
slides.say: Here is a complete worked speech. Listen for the heading first, naming the speaker, topic, time, date, occasion and venue. Then the introduction follows the six steps: it addresses the house, shows honour, welcomes the guests, states the purpose, gives the points, and shows readiness. Each body paragraph keeps audience contact, with phrases like, Mr. Chairman, and rhetorical questions such as, Am I not telling the truth? Tell me! The conclusion addresses the house again, concludes, and thanks the audience. This is how a full speech sounds from start to finish.

slides.html:
Recap: a speech has a full heading; an introduction of six steps; a body with audience contact and rhetorical questions; and a conclusion that addresses the house, concludes, and thanks the guests.

Well doneYou can now write a speech for any occasion.
slides.say: Recap. A speech has a full heading, an introduction of six steps, a body with audience contact and rhetorical questions, and a conclusion that addresses the house, concludes, and thanks the guests. Well done. You can now write a speech for any occasion.
slides.example: A speech opens by addressing the house, keeps audience contact in the body, and ends with thanks.

question.q: Write a speech (heading + introduction + one body paragraph + conclusion) on ways of ensuring discipline in your school.
question.model:
A SPEECH DELIVERED BY THE SCHOOL PREFECT ON WAYS OF ENSURING DISCIPLINE DURING THE MORNING ASSEMBLY AT THE SCHOOL PARK

Mr. Chairman, the Headmaster, Staff, Fellow Students, Ladies and Gentlemen. I consider it a great honour to address you this morning on the ways of ensuring discipline in our school. May I welcome you all. There are several ways to ensure discipline. These are clear rules, good role models, and fair punishment. Below are the details.

Mr. Chairman, the first way is to set clear rules. When everyone knows what is expected, who can claim ignorance? When the rules are written and explained, students follow them. As a result, order improves and learning flourishes. I appeal that our rules be clearly displayed for all.

Mr. Chairman, Fellow Students, Ladies and Gentlemen, to crown it all, discipline grows through clear rules, good role models, and fair punishment. Let us all play our part. Thank you.

Body keeps audience contact (rhetorical question / addressing the chair)
Conclusion addresses the house, concludes and thanks

title: Debate Writing
blurb: Argue a motion before an audience: take a stance, refute the opponent, prove your points with statistics, and ask for votes.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Take a clear stance on a motion.
  • State and refute your opponent's points.
  • Prove your points with contrast and statistics, then ask for votes.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will take a clear stance on a motion. You will state and refute your opponent's points. And you will prove your points with contrast and statistics, then ask for votes.
slides.example: A debate takes a stance on a motion, refutes the opponent, proves its points, and asks for votes.

slides.html:
A debate is an argument in speech form, meant to persuade the audience to vote for your side of the motion.

The heading is the motion, in capitals and underlined.
slides.say: A debate is an argument in speech form, meant to persuade the audience to vote for your side of the motion. The heading is the motion, written in capitals and underlined.
slides.example: A debate is an argument in speech form that persuades the audience to vote for one side of a motion.

slides.h: Five steps to open a debate
slides.html:
• Address the house.
  • Take your stand on the motion.
  • State two of your opponent's points and refute them.
  • Bring out your points.
  • Show readiness.
slides.say: There are five steps to open a debate. Address the house. Take your stand on the motion. State two of your opponent's points, and refute them. Bring out your points. And show readiness.
slides.example: Mr. Chairman, the motion before the house is that village life is better than city life. I speak for the motion.

slides.html: The motion before the house is that 'farmers contribute more to the economy than teachers'. I speak for the motion. My fellow student said teachers train labourers and pay taxes. What a falsehood! However, teachers teach only the theoretical, and their tax contribution is insignificant.
slides.say: Here is how you take a stance and refute. The motion before the house is that farmers contribute more to the economy than teachers. I speak for the motion. My fellow student said teachers train labourers and pay taxes. What a falsehood! However, teachers teach only the theoretical, and their tax contribution is insignificant.
slides.example: My opponent argued that cities offer more jobs. What a falsehood! However, the high cost of living in the city swallows those very earnings.

slides.html:
Develop each point in a paragraph, but always contrast it with the other side, proving yours is stronger. Use statistics to convince.

Contrasting transitions: however, in contrast, on the other hand, nevertheless, although.
slides.say: Develop each point in a paragraph, but always contrast it with the other side, proving yours is stronger. And use statistics to convince. Useful contrasting transitions are: however, in contrast, on the other hand, nevertheless, and although.
slides.example: Mr. Chairman, village life is healthier. The air is clean and food is fresh; in contrast, city air is choked with fumes. A 2019 health report linked city pollution to rising chest diseases.

slides.html:
Close in four moves: address the house → ask for more votes → conclude → thank the house.

From my proofs, it is clear that farmers contribute more than teachers. I therefore ask for more votes. Thank you.
slides.say: Close in four moves. Address the house. Ask for more votes. Conclude. Then thank the house. For example: From my proofs, it is clear that farmers contribute more than teachers. I therefore ask for more votes. Thank you.
slides.example: Mr. Chairman, from my proofs it is clear that village life is better than city life. I therefore ask for more votes. Thank you.

slides.h: A complete debate — study every part
slides.html:
BOARDING SCHOOL SYSTEM IS BETTER THAN DAY SCHOOL SYSTEM

Mr. Chairman, Panel of Judges, Invited Guests, Timekeepers, Co-debaters, Fellow Students, Ladies and Gentlemen. The motion before the house is that the boarding school system is better than the day school system. I speak for the motion. The first speaker argued that day school is cheaper, and that day students enjoy good nutrition because their parents cook for them. What a falsehood! However, Mr. Chairman, day school is expensive, because students spend a lot on daily transport and feeding, while their counterparts pay only feeding fees. Also, day students do not eat a well-balanced diet, since their parents are not trained nutritionists. There are several reasons boarding school is far better. The reasons are improved academic performance, better national unity, and improved moral values.

Mr. Chairman, the first reason is improved academic performance. Boarding students get more time to study without disturbance, under a well-planned, supervised arrangement. In contrast, day students study without supervision and are disturbed by errands and home entertainment. The effect is that boarding students complete the syllabus and perform better. For instance, last year's WAEC results showed that of the 100 best schools, only 2 were day schools — and they placed 99th and 100th. These facts suggest everyone should send their wards to boarding school.

Mr. Chairman, boarding schools also build better national unity. Students come from across the country, from different tribes and cultures, and as they live together they learn to respect one another's values. This helps peace prevail. In contrast, day students mingle mostly within one culture, which, if unchecked, can fuel ethnic tension. I appeal to you to send your wards to boarding school to support our unity.

Mr. Chairman, boarding schools improve moral values. Boarding students are restrained from devices that promote immorality, such as phones, which reduces harmful relationships. In contrast, day students freely use such devices, which can lower moral standards. According to a Ghana Health Service report, about 80% of students who become pregnant while schooling undergo illegal abortion, with serious consequences. We should send our children to boarding school to protect their morals.

Mr. Chairman, Panel of Judges, Co-debaters, Fellow Students, Ladies and Gentlemen, from my proofs and arguments, it is now clear that boarding schools are far better than day schools. I therefore ask for more votes.

Thank you.
slides.say: Here is a complete worked debate for the motion that boarding school is better than day school. Listen for the heading, which is the motion. The introduction addresses the house, takes a stand, states the opponent's points and refutes them with, What a falsehood, and, However, then gives the speaker's own points and readiness. Each body paragraph proves one point and contrasts it with the other side, using words like, in contrast, and supports it with a statistic. The conclusion addresses the house, asks for votes, and thanks the audience. This is a full, persuasive debate.

slides.html:
Recap: a debate's heading is the motion; the introduction takes a stance and refutes the opponent; the body proves points by contrast and statistics; the conclusion asks for votes and thanks the house.

Well doneYou can now argue a motion convincingly.
slides.say: Recap. A debate's heading is the motion. The introduction takes a stance, and refutes the opponent. The body proves your points by contrast and statistics. And the conclusion asks for votes, and thanks the house. Well done. You can now argue a motion convincingly.
slides.example: A debate states a stance, refutes the other side, proves its points by contrast, and asks for votes.

I speak for the motion. My opponent argued that day school is cheaper. However, day students spend heavily on daily transport and feeding, which often costs more in the long run.

States and refutes an opponent's point

My opponent argued that cities offer more jobs. However, with the rise of remote work, many jobs can now be done from anywhere, while the city's high cost of living swallows those very earnings.
