question.q: Write a debate (heading + introduction + one body paragraph + conclusion) FOR the motion: 'Extra classes should be banned in schools.'
question.model:
EXTRA CLASSES SHOULD BE BANNED IN SCHOOLS

Mr. Chairman, Panel of Judges, Co-debaters, Ladies and Gentlemen. The motion before the house is that 'extra classes should be banned in schools'. I speak for the motion. My opponent argued that extra classes help students cover more work. What a falsehood! However, extra classes only exhaust students and rob them of needed rest. There are several reasons extra classes should be banned. These are student fatigue, extra cost to parents, and lost family time. Below are the details.

Mr. Chairman, the first reason is student fatigue. Children already spend long hours in normal classes. Can a tired mind truly learn? Statistics from the Ghana Education Service show that overworked students perform worse in exams. In contrast, well-rested students stay sharp and focused. I therefore urge that extra classes be banned to protect our students' health.

Mr. Chairman, Co-debaters, Ladies and Gentlemen, from my proofs it is clear that extra classes do more harm than good. I therefore ask for more votes. Thank you.
rubric: Body contrasts the two sides and uses a statistic
Conclusion asks for votes and thanks the house

title: Argumentative Essays
blurb: Take a position and defend it with evidence, while addressing the opposing view.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Write an introduction with a hook, background and clear stance.
  • Build body paragraphs: claim → evidence → explanation → counter-argument.
  • Conclude by restating your stance and summarising.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will write an introduction with a hook, background, and a clear stance. You will build body paragraphs from a claim, evidence, an explanation, and a counter-argument. And you will conclude by restating your stance, and summarising.
slides.example: An argumentative essay takes a position and defends it with evidence while addressing the opposing view.

slides.html: An argumentative essay takes a position on an issue and presents evidence to support it, while also considering opposing views. The goal is to persuade through logic and well-supported arguments.
slides.say: An argumentative essay takes a position on an issue, and presents evidence to support it, while also considering opposing views. The goal is to persuade, through logic and well supported arguments.
slides.example: An argumentative essay takes a clear position on an issue and supports it with evidence and reasoning.

slides.html:
Three moves: a hook (quote, question or fact), background on the issue, and a clear stance (your debatable position).

Among all professions, teachers and farmers stand out. While teachers shape minds, it is the farmer who sustains life. Therefore, the argument leans in favour of the farmer.
slides.say: The introduction has three moves: a hook, such as a quote, a question, or a fact; some background on the issue; and a clear stance, which is your debatable position. For example: Among all professions, teachers and farmers stand out. While teachers shape minds, it is the farmer who sustains life. Therefore, the argument leans in favour of the farmer.
slides.example: Among all professions, teachers and farmers stand out. While teachers shape minds, it is the farmer who sustains life. Therefore, the argument leans in favour of the farmer.

slides.html: Each paragraph: a topic sentence, a claim, evidence (facts, statistics, examples), an explanation of how the evidence supports the claim, and a counter-argument you refute.
slides.say: Each body paragraph has: a topic sentence, a claim, evidence, such as facts, statistics, and examples, an explanation of how the evidence supports the claim, and a counter-argument, which you refute.
slides.example: Farmers are vital because they feed the nation. According to the FAO, over sixty percent of people depend on farming for a living. Without farmers, hunger would make all other progress impossible.

slides.html: Mobile phones are more relevant than televisions today because they offer greater access to information. According to a 2023 report, 85% of adults own a smartphone, and 70% use it as their main news source, while only 40% rely on television. This shows a growing reliance on phones for diverse, immediate information.
slides.say: Here is a claim with evidence in action. Mobile phones are more relevant than televisions today, because they offer greater access to information. According to a 2023 report, eighty five percent of adults own a smartphone, and seventy percent use it as their main news source, while only forty percent rely on television. This shows a growing reliance on phones, for diverse, immediate information.
slides.example: Mobile phones are more relevant than televisions today because they offer greater access to information. A 2023 report found that eighty-five percent of adults own a smartphone, while only forty percent rely on television.

slides.html:
Be clear and concise, keep a formal tone, use transitions (however, furthermore, therefore), and stay objective.

The conclusion restates your stance, summarises the points, and ends with a final thought.
slides.say: Be clear and concise, keep a formal tone, use transitions like however, furthermore, and therefore, and stay objective. The conclusion restates your stance, summarises the points, and ends with a final thought.
slides.example: In conclusion, although both are important, the farmer is more essential, for without food no other achievement, including education, would be possible.

slides.h: A complete argument — study every part
slides.html:
THE FARMER VS. THE TEACHER

In every community, different jobs play important roles in helping people live better lives. Among these, the roles of farmers and teachers are especially crucial. Farmers grow the food we eat, while teachers help us learn. Both are important, but when we consider what is most necessary for life, the farmer stands out as more important — for without food, people cannot survive, which makes all other things, including education, less possible.

To see why farmers are so important, we must understand what they do. Farmers grow and produce the food everyone needs to live. According to the Food and Agriculture Organisation, over 60% of people worldwide depend directly on farming for a living. If there were no farmers, societies would face hunger and food shortages, making healthy life impossible. Because farmers provide the food that keeps us alive, their work is essential for survival everywhere.

In addition to growing food, farmers care for the environment. They use methods such as crop rotation and organic farming to protect the land, water and air for future generations. The United Nations Environment Programme notes that sustainable farming is vital for keeping the environment safe. This work in protecting natural resources is crucial for the planet's health.

On the other hand, teachers play an important role in helping people learn and grow. Education drives personal growth and the progress of society; teachers inspire students, teach values, and give them the knowledge to succeed. A well-educated population helps a country innovate and develop. The impact of teachers on individuals and society is therefore significant.

However, education cannot happen if basic needs like food are not met. No one can learn well while hungry, and without farmers there would be no food to sustain students or schools. The work of farmers is thus the foundation that allows education to happen at all.

In conclusion, while both farmers and teachers are important, the work of farmers is more basic and necessary. Producing food is the most essential human need, and without it nothing else, including education, would be possible. Therefore, when we consider the most important job in society, the farmer plays the most vital role.
slides.say: Here is a complete worked argumentative essay arguing that the farmer is more important than the teacher. Listen for the introduction: a hook and background, then a clear, debatable stance. Each body paragraph makes a claim and supports it with evidence, including figures from the F A O and the U N Environment Programme. Notice the paragraph that fairly presents the teacher's side, then refutes it by showing that education depends on food. The conclusion restates the stance and summarises. This is how a balanced, evidence-based argument is built.

slides.html:
Recap: take a stance; introduce with hook + background + stance; build paragraphs with claim → evidence → explanation → counter-argument; conclude by restating and summarising.

Well doneYou can now argue a position with evidence.
slides.say: Recap. Take a stance. Introduce with a hook, background, and stance. Build paragraphs with a claim, evidence, an explanation, and a counter-argument. And conclude by restating, and summarising. Well done. You can now argue a position with evidence.
slides.example: An argumentative essay opens with a clear stance, defends it with evidence, refutes the counter-view, and concludes firmly.

rewrite.weak: Phones are better than TV. They do more things. TV is old. I like phones.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite as an argumentative introduction: a hook, background, and a clear, debatable stance.
rewrite.model: In the modern era, technology shapes daily life, with mobile phones and televisions among the most common devices. While both inform and entertain, their relevance differs sharply. With its versatility and portability, the mobile phone has emerged as more relevant than the television in nearly every aspect of modern life.
rubric: Mentions both sides of the issue

points.passage: Mobile phones are more relevant than televisions today because they offer greater access to information. According to a 2023 report, 85% of adults own a smartphone, and 70% use it as their primary news source, while only 40% rely on television. These statistics highlight the growing reliance on phones for immediate, diverse access to information.
model: Claim: mobile phones are more relevant than televisions
Reason: they give greater access to information
Evidence: 85% own a smartphone; 70% use it for news vs 40% for TV
Explanation: this shows growing reliance on phones
Source/date: a 2023 report

points: Stance: a balanced diet is essential
Evidence: studies link it to lower risk of heart disease
Counter: some say it is expensive — but local foods are affordable
frompoints.instruction: Write ONE argumentative body paragraph from these: topic sentence/claim → evidence → explanation → counter-argument.
frompoints.model: A balanced diet is essential because it protects the body from disease. Research consistently links a varied diet rich in fruits, vegetables and grains to a lower risk of heart disease and diabetes. This shows that what we eat directly shapes our long-term health. Some argue that healthy eating is too expensive; however, affordable local foods such as beans, kontomire and plantain provide the same nutrients at a low cost. A balanced diet is therefore within everyone's reach.
rubric: Opens with a clear claim/topic sentence
Explains how the evidence supports the claim

question.q: Write a short argumentative essay (intro + two body paragraphs + conclusion) on whether social media is more harmful than beneficial. Take a clear stance.
question.model:
Social media has woven itself into nearly every part of modern life, from how we talk to how we learn. Some see it as a dangerous distraction, others as a powerful tool. In my view, social media is more beneficial than harmful when used wisely, because it connects people and spreads knowledge.

First, social media connects people across great distances. Families separated by travel stay in touch daily through free messaging and video calls. This strengthens relationships that distance once weakened. Critics say it replaces real friendship; however, for many it deepens bonds that would otherwise fade.

Second, social media spreads useful knowledge quickly. Students follow educators and access free tutorials and current news at no cost. This widens learning beyond the classroom. While harmful content does exist, teaching responsible use addresses this far better than avoiding the platforms altogether.

In conclusion, although social media carries real risks, its power to connect people and spread knowledge makes it more beneficial than harmful. With wise use and good guidance, society can enjoy its benefits while limiting its dangers.
rubric: Introduction has hook/background and a clear stance
Each body paragraph has a claim and evidence/example
At least one counter-argument is addressed
Conclusion restates the stance and summarises

title: Minutes Writing
blurb: Record a meeting accurately: heading, attendance, agenda items, decisions, action items and adjournment.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Explain the purpose of minutes.
  • Lay out the key components: heading, attendance, agenda items, decisions, actions, adjournment.
  • Write clear, concise, accurate minutes.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will explain the purpose of minutes. You will lay out the key components: the heading, attendance, agenda items, decisions, actions, and adjournment. And you will write clear, concise, accurate minutes.
slides.example: Minutes record a meeting's attendance, discussions, decisions, and action items accurately.

slides.html: Minutes are the official written record of a meeting — a summary of what was discussed, decided and assigned. They provide documentation, reference, accountability and transparency.
slides.say: Minutes are the official written record of a meeting. They are a summary of what was discussed, decided, and assigned. They provide documentation, reference, accountability, and transparency.
slides.example: Minutes are the official written record of what a meeting discussed, decided, and assigned.

slides.html: Start with the heading: meeting title, date, time, location. Then the attendance: those present (with positions) and any absentees.
slides.say: Start with the heading: the meeting title, the date, the time, and the location. Then the attendance: those present, with their positions, and any absentees.
slides.example: Minutes of the Science Club Meeting. Date: 10th March 2025. Time: 3:30 p.m. Venue: Laboratory 2. Present: 15 members. Absent: Yaw Boateng (excused).

slides.html: Note the approval of previous minutes (and who moved/seconded). Then each agenda item records: the discussion, the decision, and the action items (who does what, by when).
slides.say: Note the approval of the previous minutes, and who moved and seconded it. Then, for each agenda item, record the discussion, the decision, and the action items, that is, who does what, and by when.
slides.example: The previous minutes were read and approved, moved by Ama Owusu and seconded by Esi Tetteh.

slides.html: End with other business, the date of the next meeting, and the adjournment (the time the meeting closed). Sign off: prepared by, approved by.
slides.say: End with any other business, the date of the next meeting, and the adjournment, that is, the time the meeting closed. Then sign off: prepared by, and approved by.
slides.example: Under any other business, members agreed to plan a science fair. The next meeting was fixed for 24th March, and the meeting was adjourned at 4:30 p.m.

slides.html: Be clear and concise; use a structured format; record key points (decisions and actions); use the active voice; and proofread for accuracy.
slides.say: Be clear and concise. Use a structured format. Record the key points, which are the decisions and the actions. Use the active voice. And proofread for accuracy.
slides.example: Discussion: members noted the classroom is too hot. Decision: a standing fan will be bought. Action: the treasurer to collect contributions by Friday.

slides.html:
Recap: minutes record a meeting via heading → attendance → approval → agenda items (discussion, decision, actions) → other business → next meeting → adjournment. Keep them clear, accurate and concise.

Well doneYou can now take minutes of any meeting.
slides.say: Recap. Minutes record a meeting through the heading, the attendance, the approval of previous minutes, the agenda items with their discussion, decision, and actions, any other business, the next meeting, and the adjournment. Keep them clear, accurate, and concise. Well done. You can now take minutes of any meeting.
slides.example: Good minutes record the heading, attendance, decisions, and actions of a meeting clearly and accurately.

rewrite.weak: we met and talked about the playground and decided to fix it and some people will help
rewrite.hint: Rewrite as a proper minutes agenda item with Discussion, Decision and Action Items (name responsible people).
rewrite.model:
3.2 Enhancing School Facilities
Discussion: Ms. Adams proposed revamping the school playground and adding outdoor learning spaces.
Decision: The PTA agreed to form a committee to explore funding and design.
Action Items: Ms. Adams, Mrs. Davis and Mr. Wilson to prepare a costed plan by the next meeting.
rubric: Lists Action Items with responsible persons

points.passage: Minutes of the Annual PTA Meeting. Date: August 5, 2024. Time: 6:00–8:00 PM. Location: School Auditorium. Present: Mr. Mensah (President), Mrs. Davis (Secretary), and others. The previous minutes were approved unanimously. The PTA agreed to form a committee for a playground upgrade. The meeting was adjourned at 8:00 PM.
model: Heading: PTA meeting, date, time, location
Attendance: President, Secretary and others
Previous minutes approved unanimously
A decision: form a committee for the playground upgrade
Adjournment: 8:00 PM

points: Discussion: the room is too hot
Decision: collect GHS 5 from each student
Action: treasurer to collect by Friday
frompoints.instruction: Write this as a proper minutes agenda item (Discussion, Decision, Action Items).
frompoints.model:
Item 1: Purchase of a Class Fan
Discussion: Members noted that the classroom becomes very hot in the afternoons, making study difficult.
Decision: The class agreed to buy a standing fan, funded by a contribution of GHS 5 from each student.
Action Items: The class treasurer to collect the contributions and report by Friday.
rubric: Action Items name who and by when

question.q: Write a short set of minutes (heading, attendance, one agenda item with discussion/decision/action, adjournment) for a school club meeting.
question.model:
Minutes of the Science Club Meeting
Date: 10th March 2025
Time: 3:30–4:30 PM
Location: Laboratory 2

Attendance: Kofi Mensah (President), Ama Owusu (Secretary), 14 members present. Absent: Yaw Boateng (excused).

1. Approval of Previous Minutes
The minutes of the previous meeting were read and approved, moved by Ama Owusu and seconded by Esi Tetteh.

2. Agenda Item: Science Fair Project
Discussion: Members discussed building a low-cost water filter for the regional science fair.
Decision: The club agreed to build the filter using local materials.
Action Items: Kofi Mensah to source materials; Ama Owusu to write the report, both by 24th March.

3. Adjournment
The meeting was adjourned at 4:30 PM.

Prepared by: Ama Owusu (Secretary)
rubric: Heading with title, date, time, location
Agenda item with discussion, decision, action items

title: Informal Letters & Dialogue
blurb: Write a friendly letter to someone close — conversational tone, the right format, and how to set out dialogue.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Recognise when a letter is informal.
  • Lay out the format: address, date, salutation, body, subscription, first name.
  • Write in a warm, conversational tone, and set out a dialogue.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will recognise when a letter is informal. You will lay out the format: the address, date, salutation, body, subscription, and first name. And you will write in a warm, conversational tone, and set out a dialogue.
slides.example: An informal letter is written warmly to someone close, in a relaxed, conversational tone.

slides.html: An informal letter is written to people very close to you — friends, family, pen pals. The language is conversational: you may use slang and idioms, as if on a phone call to a close friend.
slides.say: An informal letter is written to people very close to you: friends, family, or pen pals. The language is conversational. You may use slang and idioms, as if you were on a phone call to a close friend.
slides.example: An informal letter is written to people close to you, such as friends and family, in a friendly tone.

slides.html:
Features in order: sender's address → date → salutation → body (introduction, main body, conclusion) → subscription → first name.

SalutationWarm: 'Dear Ama,' 'My dear friend,'
slides.say: The features in order are: the sender's address, the date, the salutation, the body, which has an introduction, main body, and conclusion, the subscription, and your first name. The salutation is warm, such as: Dear Ama, or, my dear friend.
slides.example: P. O. Box 12, Kumasi. 10th January 2025. Dear Abena, ... Your friend, Afia.

slides.html: Ask after the reader, share your news warmly, and use a relaxed tone. The subscription is friendly: 'Your friend,' 'Yours affectionately,' then your first name only.
slides.say: Ask after the reader, share your news warmly, and use a relaxed tone. The subscription is friendly, such as: your friend, or, yours affectionately, followed by your first name only.
slides.example: How are you, my dear friend? I hope you and your family are doing well. I have such exciting news to share with you!

slides.html:
A dialogue is a written conversation. Put each speaker's name, then a colon, then their words, on a new line for each speaker.

Ama: Good morning, Kofi. Did you finish the homework?
Kofi: Almost! I have one more question to solve.
slides.say: A dialogue is a written conversation. Put each speaker's name, then a colon, then their words, on a new line for each speaker. For example. Ama: Good morning, Kofi. Did you finish the homework? Kofi: Almost! I have one more question to solve.
slides.example: Ama: Good morning, Kofi. Did you finish the homework? Kofi: Almost! I have just one more question to solve.

slides.html:
Recap: an informal letter goes to someone close, in a conversational tone, with the format address → date → salutation → body → subscription → first name. A dialogue labels each speaker and starts a new line per turn.

Well doneYou can now write a warm letter and set out a dialogue.
slides.say: Recap. An informal letter goes to someone close, in a conversational tone, with the format: address, date, salutation, body, subscription, and first name. A dialogue labels each speaker, and starts a new line for each turn. Well done. You can now write a warm letter, and set out a dialogue.
slides.example: An informal letter keeps a warm, conversational tone and follows the friendly letter format.

rewrite.weak: Dear Sir, I am writing to formally inform you that I shall be visiting during the vacation. Yours faithfully.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite this in a warm, INFORMAL tone for a close friend (relaxed words, friendly salutation and subscription).
rewrite.model:
Dear Kofi,

How are you doing, my friend? I can't wait to tell you — I'm coming to spend the holidays with you! We'll have so much fun catching up. See you soon!

Your friend,
Yaw

points.passage:
Dear Ama,

It's been so long! How is everyone at home? I'm writing to share my exciting news — I came first in class this term! I worked really hard and it paid off. I hope to see you during the holidays so we can celebrate together.

Your friend,
Esi
model: This is an informal letter to a close friend
Salutation is warm ('Dear Ama')
The news: the writer came first in class
Tone is conversational and friendly
Closes with 'Your friend' + first name only

points: News: you have moved to a new school
Feeling: a little nervous but happy
frompoints.instruction: Write an informal letter body (3–4 sentences) from these points, in a warm, conversational tone.
frompoints.model: Guess what? I've just moved to a brand-new school, and so far I love it! The teachers are really friendly and patient, which has made settling in much easier. I'll admit I was a little nervous at first, but now I'm genuinely happy here. You simply must come and visit so I can show you around!
rubric: Covers the news and the detail

question.q: Write an informal letter to a friend telling them about how you spent your last holiday. Use the correct informal format.
question.model:
P.O. Box 12,
Kumasi.
10th January 2025

Dear Abena,

How are you, my dear friend? I hope you and your family are doing well. I've been dying to tell you about my amazing holiday!

I spent most of it at my grandmother's village in the Volta Region. Every morning we went to the farm, and in the evenings she told us the funniest old stories around the fire. I even learned to weave a small basket! The best part was the festival — the drumming and dancing went on late into the night.

I wish you had been there to enjoy it with me. Next time, you simply must come along!

Do write back and tell me about your own holiday. Take care of yourself.

Your friend,
Afia
rubric: Tells the holiday story with vivid detail

title: Report Writing
blurb: Give a clear, factual account of an event or incident: who, what, when, where — objectively.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Explain the purpose of a report.
  • Include the key elements: date, time, location, people involved, description.
  • Write objectively, with clarity and accuracy.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will explain the purpose of a report. You will include the key elements: the date, time, location, people involved, and a description. And you will write objectively, with clarity and accuracy.
slides.example: A report gives a clear, factual account of an event: when, where, who, and what happened.

slides.html: A report recounts an event or incident, giving a detailed, factual outcome. We write reports to narrate what happened so it can be investigated, acted on, or kept for reference.
slides.say: A report recounts an event or incident, giving a detailed, factual outcome. We write reports to narrate what happened, so it can be investigated, acted on, or kept for reference.
slides.example: A report recounts an event or incident factually, so it can be acted on or kept for reference.

slides.html:
The introduction summarises the whole report and contains its key facts.

On the evening of 12th August 2024, at about 8:15 p.m., I witnessed an armed robbery at the Das Mall on Main Street, Adum.
slides.say: The introduction summarises the whole report, and contains its key facts. For example: On the evening of the twelfth of August 2024, at about eight fifteen p m, I witnessed an armed robbery at the Das Mall, on Main Street, Adum.
slides.example: On Friday, 15th March 2025, at about 8:00 a.m., a fire broke out in the storeroom of Block B at Unity School.

slides.html:
• Date, time and location — when and where.
  • People involved — including witnesses.
  • Incident description — a clear account of the event.
slides.say: A report must include these key elements. The date, time, and location: when and where it happened. The people involved, including any witnesses. And the incident description: a clear account of the event.
slides.example: On Monday at about 7:00 a.m., near the school gate, a taxi knocked down a cyclist, who was taken to hospital.

slides.html: Stick to the facts as observed — no personal opinion or assumption. Write clearly and accurately so the report is easy to understand and use.
slides.say: Stick to the facts as observed, with no personal opinion, and no assumption. And write clearly and accurately, so the report is easy to understand, and to use.
slides.example: Thick smoke was seen coming from the window, and the students were quickly and calmly evacuated to the assembly ground.

slides.html:
Recap: a report narrates an event factually. The introduction summarises it; include the date, time, location, people and description; stay objective, clear and accurate.

Well doneYou can now write a clear report of any incident.
slides.say: Recap. A report narrates an event factually. The introduction summarises it. Include the date, time, location, people, and the description. And stay objective, clear, and accurate. Well done. You can now write a clear report of any incident.
slides.example: A report states the date, time, location, people, and a factual account of an event, without opinion.

rewrite.weak: It was so scary! There was a terrible fire and I think everyone was really frightened and it was just awful.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite as an objective report opening: state date, time, location and what happened — no emotion words.
rewrite.model: On the afternoon of 3rd June 2024, at about 2:00 p.m., a fire broke out in the storeroom of Block B at Unity School. Thick smoke was seen coming from the window, and students were quickly evacuated to the assembly ground.

points.passage: On the evening of 12th August 2024, at approximately 8:15 PM, I witnessed an armed robbery at the Das Mall on Main Street, Adum. Two masked individuals, both armed with handguns, forced their way into the premises and demanded money from the cashier. I observed the events from a safe distance.
model: Date and time: 12th August 2024, about 8:15 PM
Location: Das Mall, Main Street, Adum
People involved: two masked, armed robbers; the cashier; the witness
What happened: the robbers forced in and demanded money
Tone: objective, factual

points: What: a taxi knocked a cyclist
Outcome: cyclist injured, taken to hospital
frompoints.instruction: Write a short, objective report introduction from these points.
frompoints.model: On Monday at about 7:00 a.m., a road accident occurred near the school gate. A taxi travelling towards town knocked down a cyclist who was crossing the road. The cyclist sustained injuries to the leg and was taken to the nearby hospital for treatment.

question.q: Write a short report of an event you witnessed at your school (e.g. a sports day or a fire drill). Include date, time, location, people and a factual description.
question.model:
REPORT ON THE INTER-HOUSE SPORTS DAY

On Friday, 15th March 2025, the annual inter-house sports competition was held at the school park from 8:00 a.m. to 1:00 p.m. All four houses — Red, Blue, Green and Yellow — took part, together with teachers, parents and invited guests.

The events began with a march-past, followed by track races, the long jump and a relay. Blue House won the 100-metre race, while Green House dominated the relay events. The tug-of-war drew the loudest cheers from the crowd.

By the end of the day, Blue House had emerged overall champions with 145 points. The headmaster presented the trophy and thanked everyone for their orderly conduct. The event ended peacefully at 1:00 p.m.
rubric: Describes the events factually and in order

title: Descriptive & Story Writing
blurb: Paint vivid pictures and tell engaging stories using the senses, the right tense, and expressive language.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Tell narration apart from description, and use the right tense.
  • Describe with the five senses.
  • Use expressive idioms to convey emotion in a story.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will tell narration apart from description, and use the right tense. You will describe with the five senses. And you will use expressive idioms to convey emotion in a story.
slides.example: Description paints a scene with the senses; narration tells a past event in time order.

slides.html: Narration gives an account of a past event — so it uses the past tense. Description gives a vivid account of people, places or events, usually in the present tense, using the five senses.
slides.say: Narration gives an account of a past event, so it uses the past tense. Description gives a vivid account of people, places, or events, usually in the present tense, using the five senses.
slides.example: The market hummed with life as traders called out their prices and the smoky scent of grilled plantain filled the air.

slides.html:
Bring a scene alive with sight, sound, smell, taste and touch, and strong adjectives.

The beach at sunset is breathtaking. The sky glows orange and pink, gentle waves whisper on the shore, and the cool breeze carries the scent of saltwater.
slides.say: Bring a scene alive with sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch, and with strong adjectives. For example: The beach at sunset is breathtaking. The sky glows orange and pink, gentle waves whisper on the shore, and the cool breeze carries the scent of saltwater.
slides.example: The beach at sunset is breathtaking: the sky glows orange, gentle waves whisper on the shore, and the cool breeze carries the scent of saltwater.

slides.html:
Use vivid idioms to show emotion instead of just naming it:

  • Joy: tears of joy streamed down her cheeks; she was floating in the air.
  • Shock: his heart skipped a beat; she froze in her tracks.
slides.say: Use vivid idioms to show emotion, instead of just naming it. For joy: tears of joy streamed down her cheeks; or, she was floating in the air. For shock: his heart skipped a beat; or, she froze in her tracks.
slides.example: When the results came, tears of joy streamed down her cheeks, and for a moment she felt she was floating in the air.

slides.html: A story has a clear beginning, middle and end, with characters, a setting and a plot (often a problem that gets resolved). Keep events in time order and stay in the past tense.
slides.say: A story has a clear beginning, middle, and end, with characters, a setting, and a plot, often a problem that gets resolved. Keep your events in time order, and stay in the past tense.
slides.example: It was meant to be an ordinary Saturday. Suddenly, a sharp cry froze me in my tracks, and I rushed to help before I even knew what I was doing.

slides.html:
Recap: narration = past tense; description = present tense + the five senses. Use expressive idioms to show emotion, and tell a story with a clear beginning, middle and end.

Well doneYou can now paint a scene and tell a gripping story.
slides.say: Recap. Narration uses the past tense. Description uses the present tense, and the five senses. Use expressive idioms to show emotion, and tell a story with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Well done. You can now paint a scene, and tell a gripping story.
slides.example: Use the senses to describe and time order to narrate, in the right tense, to bring writing alive.

rewrite.weak: The market was busy. There were people. It was loud. I liked it.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite as a vivid DESCRIPTION using at least three senses and strong adjectives.
rewrite.model: The market hummed like a giant beehive. Traders shouted their prices over the clatter of trays, while the warm air swirled with the smoky scent of grilled plantain and ripe mangoes. Bright cloths and glistening tomatoes caught the eye at every turn — the whole place pulsed with life.
rubric: At least one figure of speech (simile/metaphor)

points.passage: Tears of joy streamed down Ama's cheeks as she read the letter. Her hands trembled and her heart raced. She had won the national essay prize! For a moment she simply floated, unable to believe the wonderful news.
model: This is narrative (past tense) with descriptive emotion
The event: Ama won the national essay prize
Emotion shown, not just named: 'tears of joy streamed down', 'heart raced', 'floated'
Tense used: past tense
It uses expressive idioms to show feeling

frompoints.instruction: Write ONE descriptive paragraph (present tense) using these sensory points.
frompoints.model: The rainy morning wraps the town in soft grey light. Puddles shimmer across the road, trembling with every fresh drop, while the rain drums a steady rhythm on the tin roofs. The air is rich with the earthy smell of wet soil, cool and damp against the skin. It is the kind of morning that makes the whole world feel calm and newly washed.
rubric: Uses sight, sound, smell and touch
Reads smoothly with a clear impression

question.q: Write a short story (5–8 sentences) about a day something unexpected happened. Use the past tense, time order, and at least one expressive idiom.
question.model: It was meant to be an ordinary Saturday. That morning, I set off early to fetch water from the stream, humming as I walked. Suddenly, a sharp cry froze me in my tracks — a small boy had slipped into the fast-flowing water. My heart skipped a beat. Without thinking, I dropped my bucket and waded in, gripping his arm until I dragged him safely to the bank. By the time his mother arrived, tears of relief were streaming down her cheeks. That day I learned that courage often arrives before we are ready for it.
rubric: Events follow a clear time order
Has a clear beginning, problem and resolution
Uses at least one expressive idiom

title: Creative & Imaginative Writing
blurb: Write with imagination, a personal voice, vivid characters and originality.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Use imagination to invent worlds, ideas and language.
  • Develop a personal voice and style.
  • Build believable characters.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will use imagination to invent worlds, ideas, and language. You will develop a personal voice and style. And you will build believable characters.
slides.example: Creative writing uses imagination, a personal voice, and well-developed characters.

slides.html: Creative writing is imaginative writing that goes beyond formal forms — short stories, poems, descriptive prose. It values originality and creativity, letting you express thoughts and emotions in artistic ways.
slides.say: Creative writing is imaginative writing that goes beyond formal forms. It includes short stories, poems, and descriptive prose. It values originality and creativity, letting you express your thoughts and emotions in artistic ways.
slides.example: Creative writing is imaginative writing, such as stories and poems, that values originality.

slides.html: Imagination is the engine of creative writing. Invent whole worlds, explore unusual themes, and even play with language itself — new words, fresh images, unexpected structures.
slides.say: Imagination is the engine of creative writing. Invent whole worlds, explore unusual themes, and even play with language itself, using new words, fresh images, and unexpected structures.
slides.example: The village of Nkwantanan existed only where two rivers argued, shimmering by day and whispering secrets by night.

slides.html: Your voice is how you 'speak' on the page — humorous, serious, reflective. Your style is how you use language — lush and descriptive, or spare and minimal. Aim for authenticity.
slides.say: Your voice is how you speak on the page: humorous, serious, or reflective. Your style is how you use language: lush and descriptive, or spare and minimal. Above all, aim for authenticity, so your true voice shines through.
slides.example: Old Kwabena lived alone at the edge of the village, in a mud house worn smooth by decades of patient rain.

slides.html: Characters are the soul of a story. Make them believable: give them appearance, actions, thoughts, feelings, wants and flaws — so readers care about them.
slides.say: Characters are the soul of a story. Make them believable. Give them an appearance, actions, thoughts, feelings, wants, and flaws, so that readers come to care about them.
slides.example: Adwoa was the kind of girl who walked towards mysteries, not away from them, with a sharp mind and a fearless heart.

slides.html:
Recap: creative writing prizes imagination, a genuine personal voice and style, and well-developed characters. Write with originality and let your true voice show.

Well doneYou can now write with imagination and a voice of your own.
slides.say: Recap. Creative writing prizes imagination, a genuine personal voice and style, and well developed characters. Write with originality, and let your true voice show. Well done. You can now write with imagination, and a voice of your own.
slides.example: Creative writing prizes imagination, a genuine voice, and characters the reader comes to care about.

rewrite.weak: There was a man. He was old. He lived in a house. He was lonely.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite imaginatively: bring the character alive with vivid detail, voice and feeling.
rewrite.model: Old Kwabena lived alone at the edge of the village, in a mud house worn smooth by decades of rain. Each evening he sat on his crooked stool, watching the road as though waiting for a visitor who never came. His eyes, dim but kind, held a thousand untold stories — and a loneliness he wore as quietly as his faded cloth.
rubric: The character is brought to life with vivid detail
Shows feeling rather than just naming it

points.passage: Old Kwabena lived alone at the edge of the village, in a mud house worn smooth by years of rain. Each evening he sat on his crooked stool, watching the road as if waiting for someone who never came. His dim but kind eyes held a thousand untold stories.
model: This is creative/imaginative writing (character description)
The character: Old Kwabena, who lives alone
Vivid details: mud house, crooked stool, dim kind eyes
Feeling shown: loneliness and longing
It has a distinct, expressive voice

points: Want: to find a hidden waterfall
frompoints.instruction: Write an imaginative opening (3–4 sentences) introducing this character and setting.
frompoints.model: Adwoa was the kind of girl who walked towards mysteries, not away from them. With a sharp mind and a heart that knew no fear, she slipped into the whispering forest where the older children dared not go. Somewhere beyond the tangle of vines, she was certain, lay the hidden waterfall the elders only spoke of in stories — and today, she meant to find it.
rubric: Shows the character's trait through action

question.q: Write the opening of an imaginative story (5–7 sentences) set in a place of your own invention. Create a vivid character and a strong sense of voice.
question.model: The village of Nkwantanan existed only where two rivers argued. By day it shimmered like any other place — children chasing goats, women pounding fufu, smoke curling lazily into the sky. But Kojo knew its secret. Every night, when the moon rose full and silver, the two rivers fell silent, and a third path appeared between them, leading somewhere no map had ever dared to draw. Tonight, with his grandmother's old lantern swinging in his hand, Kojo finally decided to follow it. His heart drummed with equal parts terror and delight. Whatever waited at the end of that moonlit path, he was ready to meet it.

title: Answering Comprehension
blurb: A smart, time-saving method for answering comprehension questions paragraph by paragraph.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Use a fast, paragraph-by-paragraph method to answer comprehension.
  • Answer in your own words, briefly and correctly.
  • Avoid common comprehension mistakes.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will use a fast, paragraph by paragraph method to answer comprehension. You will answer in your own words, briefly and correctly. And you will avoid common comprehension mistakes.
slides.example: Comprehension is answered fastest by working question by question through the passage.

slides.html: A comprehension gives a passage with questions. Often, the questions follow the order of the paragraphs — the answer to question 1 is usually in paragraph 1, and so on.
slides.say: A comprehension gives a passage with questions. Often, the questions follow the order of the paragraphs. The answer to question one is usually in paragraph one, and so on.
slides.example: Often the answer to question one is in paragraph one, the answer to question two in paragraph two, and so on.

slides.html:
1. Read the first question to know what it asks.
  2. Read the paragraphs until you locate the answer; underline it.
  3. Write your answer; check for grammar errors.
  4. Repeat for the rest.
WhyYou answer as you go, so you don't run out of time reading the whole passage many times.
slides.say: Here is the smart method. First, read the first question, to know what it asks. Second, read the paragraphs until you locate the answer, and underline it. Third, write your answer, and check for grammar errors. Then repeat for the rest of the questions. Why does this help? You answer as you go, so you do not run out of time reading the whole passage many times.
slides.example: Read the question first, then search the passage for the answer, underline it, and write it in your own words.

slides.html:
• The answer need not be a full sentence.
  • Do not repeat the question in your answer.
The reason the boy died is that he died through a motor accident.
He died through a motor accident.
slides.say: Two rules for your answers. First, the answer need not be a full sentence. Second, do not repeat the question in your answer. For example, instead of: the reason the boy died is that he died through a motor accident, simply write: he died through a motor accident.
slides.example: Question: Why was the boy late? Answer: He missed the bus.

slides.html: Where asked, put the idea in your own words rather than copying the passage wholesale. For 'find a word that means…', give the exact word from the text.
slides.say: Where you are asked to, put the idea in your own words, rather than copying the passage wholesale. But for a question like, find a word that means something, give the exact word from the text.
slides.example: Trees are important because they give us oxygen, provide shade, and hold the soil in place.

slides.html:
Recap: questions usually follow the paragraph order. Read the question, locate and underline the answer, write it briefly without repeating the question, and move on. Answer in your own words where asked.

Well doneYou can now tackle comprehension quickly and accurately.
slides.say: Recap. The questions usually follow the paragraph order. Read the question, locate and underline the answer, write it briefly without repeating the question, and move on. And answer in your own words where asked. Well done. You can now tackle comprehension quickly, and accurately.
slides.example: Answer comprehension question by question, briefly, in your own words, without repeating the question.

rewrite.weak: Q: Why was the boy late? A: The reason why the boy was late to school is that the boy was late because he missed the bus.
rewrite.hint: Rewrite the answer correctly: do not repeat the question, and keep it brief.
rewrite.model: He missed the bus.
rubric: Is brief and to the point

points.passage:
Trees are vital to our planet because they produce oxygen. Through photosynthesis, they absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen. They also provide shade and shelter for animals, and prevent soil erosion by holding the soil with their roots.

Question 1: Why are trees important?
Question 2: Name two other benefits of trees besides producing oxygen.
model: Q1 answer (from paragraph): trees are important because they produce oxygen
Q2 answer: they provide shade/shelter for animals; they prevent soil erosion
Note: answers come from the relevant part of the passage
Answers are brief and do not repeat the question

points: Passage idea: exercise keeps the body healthy by strengthening the heart and muscles
Question: How does exercise keep the body healthy?
frompoints.instruction: Write a correct comprehension answer (brief, no question repeated) to the question.
frompoints.model: By strengthening the heart and muscles.

question.q:
Read this short passage and answer the question.

'Kofi worked hard all year. He woke early to study and helped his mother at the market after school. When the results came, he had passed with distinction.'

Question: What two things did Kofi do that led to his success?
question.model: He woke early to study, and he helped his mother at the market after school. (Both answers are taken directly from the passage, stated briefly without repeating the question.)
rubric: Locates the answer in the passage

passages.title: The Hardworking Ant
passages.text: In a small garden behind a school in Kumasi lived a colony of ants. Among them was a tiny ant named Kofi, known for never resting during the dry season. While other insects played in the sun, Kofi carried grains of food, one by one, into the colony's store. The grasshopper next door laughed at him and spent his days singing instead of gathering food. When the heavy rains finally came, the grasshopper had nothing to eat and grew weak with hunger. Kofi, however, had a full store and shared a little with his hungry neighbour. From that day, the grasshopper understood that hard work in good times prepares us for hard times.
questions: Where did the colony of ants live?
What did Kofi do while the other insects played?
Why did the grasshopper grow weak when the rains came?
What lesson did the grasshopper finally learn?
answers: In a small garden behind a school in Kumasi.
He carried grains of food, one by one, into the colony's store.
Because he had not gathered any food and had nothing to eat.
That hard work in good times prepares us for hard times.

passages.title: Market Day at Makola
passages.text: Every Saturday, Auntie Adjoa wakes before dawn to prepare for market day at Makola. She loads her basins of ripe tomatoes, fresh pepper and garden eggs onto a truck and travels from her village into the busy heart of Accra. By six o'clock the market is alive with the calls of traders and the bargaining of customers. Auntie Adjoa greets her regular buyers warmly, for she believes a kind word sells faster than a low price. Though the work is tiring and the sun is fierce, she returns home each evening with an empty basin and a full purse, grateful for another good day.
questions: On what day does Auntie Adjoa go to the market?
Name two of the items she sells.
Why does she greet her buyers warmly?
How do we know she had a successful day?
answers: On Saturdays.
Ripe tomatoes, fresh pepper and garden eggs (any two).
Because she believes a kind word sells faster than a low price.
She returned home with an empty basin and a full purse.

passages.title: The Power of Trees
passages.text: Trees are among the most valuable gifts of nature. They release the oxygen that humans and animals need to breathe, and they absorb the carbon dioxide that pollutes the air. Their roots hold the soil firmly, preventing the erosion that washes away farmland during heavy rains. Many communities also depend on trees for fruit, firewood and shade from the burning sun. Sadly, careless cutting of trees for timber and charcoal is destroying our forests. Unless we plant more than we cut, the land will grow bare and the climate will suffer.
questions: What gas do trees release that living things need?
How do the roots of trees protect farmland?
Give two ways communities depend on trees.
What does the writer say will happen if we keep cutting trees carelessly?
answers: Oxygen.
Their roots hold the soil firmly and prevent erosion.
For fruit, firewood and shade (any two).
The land will grow bare and the climate will suffer.

passages.title: Ama's Difficult Choice
passages.text: Ama had studied hard all year for the regional spelling competition. On the morning of the contest, her younger brother fell ill and their mother had to rush him to the clinic. Someone had to stay home to mind the house and the cooking. Ama looked at her competition badge, then at the locked front door, and made her decision. She stayed behind. Though she missed her chance to compete, her teacher visited that evening and told her that her sense of responsibility had taught the whole class a greater lesson than any trophy could.
questions: What had Ama been preparing for all year?
Why did someone need to stay at home that morning?
What decision did Ama make?
According to her teacher, what did Ama's choice teach the class?
answers: The regional spelling competition.
Because her brother was ill and her mother had to rush him to the clinic, so someone had to mind the house.
She stayed at home instead of going to compete.
That her sense of responsibility taught a greater lesson than any trophy.

passages.title: The River That Changed
passages.text: When Yaw was a boy, the river near his village ran clear, and children swam in it after school. Fish were plentiful, and the women fetched clean water for cooking. Years later, illegal mining began upstream. Men dug into the riverbanks in search of gold, and mud and chemicals poured into the water. Slowly the river turned brown, the fish vanished, and the villagers fell sick from drinking it. Now an old man, Yaw warns the young ones that what took nature a lifetime to build was destroyed in only a few greedy years.
questions: What was the river like when Yaw was a boy?
What activity began upstream years later?
What two things polluted the river?
What warning does the old Yaw give the young ones?
answers: It ran clear, and children swam in it; fish were plentiful and the water was clean.
Illegal mining (galamsey).
Mud and chemicals.
That what nature took a lifetime to build was destroyed in only a few greedy years.

passages.title: A Lesson in Honesty
passages.text: On his way to school, Kwame found a brown envelope lying by the roadside. Inside was a large sum of money and a national identity card bearing the owner's name and address. Kwame's family was poor, and the money could have bought many things he longed for. Yet he remembered his grandmother's words: that a good name is worth more than gold. After school, he traced the address and returned the envelope to its grateful owner, a trader who had been weeping over his lost savings. The trader rewarded Kwame, but the boy treasured his clear conscience far more than the reward.
questions: What did Kwame find by the roadside?
What was inside the envelope?
Whose words did Kwame remember, and what did they say?
What did Kwame treasure more than the reward?
answers: A brown envelope.
A large sum of money and a national identity card.
His grandmother's; she said a good name is worth more than gold.
His clear conscience.

passages.title: The Value of Reading
passages.text: Reading is one of the surest paths to knowledge and success. Through books, a young person in a quiet village can travel to distant lands, meet great thinkers, and learn skills that no classroom alone can teach. Reading widens vocabulary, sharpens the imagination, and improves one's own writing. It also offers a peaceful escape from the worries of daily life. Those who form the habit of reading early are often the ones who speak well, write well, and think clearly. A nation of readers is a nation that grows.
questions: What does the writer call one of the surest paths to success?
How can books help a young person in a quiet village?
Give two benefits of reading mentioned in the passage.
What kind of nation does the writer say grows?
answers: Reading.
Through books they can travel to distant lands, meet great thinkers and learn new skills.
It widens vocabulary and sharpens the imagination (any two; also improves writing, offers escape).
A nation of readers.

passages.title: The Stubborn Goat
passages.text: Mr. Tetteh owned a goat that was famous in the village for its stubbornness. Each morning he tied it under the mango tree, and each morning it chewed through the rope and wandered into the neighbours' farms. The angry neighbours complained again and again. One day, instead of a rope, Mr. Tetteh built a strong wooden pen and gave the goat plenty of grass and water inside it. To his surprise, the goat settled down quietly. He realised that the animal had not been wicked at all; it had only been hungry and bored. Sometimes, he thought, the fault lies with the keeper, not the kept.
questions: What was Mr. Tetteh's goat famous for?
What did the goat do every morning?
What did Mr. Tetteh build instead of using a rope?
What did Mr. Tetteh finally realise about the goat?
answers: Its stubbornness.
It chewed through the rope and wandered into the neighbours' farms.
A strong wooden pen.
That the goat was not wicked, only hungry and bored — the fault lay with the keeper.

passages.title: The Flood
passages.text: The rains had fallen without stopping for three days. In the low-lying part of town, the gutters were choked with plastic waste and could not carry the water away. By the third night, the streets had become rivers, and water crept into homes, ruining furniture and food. Families climbed onto rooftops and called for help as their belongings floated away. When the sun finally returned, the people gathered to clean the drains they had once ignored. The disaster taught them a costly lesson about the danger of careless dumping.
questions: How long had the rains fallen without stopping?
Why could the gutters not carry the water away?
What did the families do as the water rose?
What lesson did the disaster teach the people?
answers: For three days.
Because they were choked with plastic waste.
They climbed onto rooftops and called for help.
A costly lesson about the danger of careless dumping.

passages.title: The Young Inventor
passages.text: Esi was only fifteen when she built her first machine, a simple device that warned her family whenever rainwater filled their storage barrel. She had no expensive parts, only wires, a small bell and ideas gathered from library books. Her neighbours, who once doubted that a girl could be an engineer, now came to admire her work. Encouraged by her science teacher, Esi entered a national competition and won first place. She told the reporters that curiosity, not money, is the true beginning of every invention. Her dream, she said, was to build machines that solve the everyday problems of ordinary people.
questions: How old was Esi when she built her first machine?
What did her first machine do?
Where did she get the ideas for her invention?
According to Esi, what is the true beginning of every invention?
answers: Fifteen years old.
It warned her family whenever rainwater filled their storage barrel.
From library books.
Curiosity, not money.

title: Summary Writing
blurb: Shorten a text to its key points in simple sentences — no colouring words, nothing added or removed.

slides.html:
By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Explain what a summary is.
  • Find the topic sentence / point of each paragraph.
  • Write summary answers as plain noun + action + noun sentences.
slides.say: Welcome. By the end of this module, you will explain what a summary is. You will find the topic sentence, or point, of each paragraph. And you will write summary answers as plain noun, action, noun sentences.
slides.example: A summary captures each paragraph's point in a short, simple sentence.

slides.html: A summary is the shortened version of a text. At this level, a sentence summary. Since a paragraph's topic sentence is its summary, in summary writing we hunt for the topic sentences or points.
slides.say: A summary is the shortened version of a text. At this level, it is a sentence summary. Since a paragraph's topic sentence is its summary, in summary writing we hunt for the topic sentences, or points.
slides.example: A summary is the shortened version of a text, capturing only its main points.

slides.h: The shape of a summary sentence
slides.html:
A summary answer is a simple sentence: noun (subject) + action (verb) + noun (object). It must contain all the paragraph's information without adding or removing anything.

Poverty causes teenage pregnancy.
slides.say: A summary answer is a simple sentence: a noun, that is the subject, plus an action, that is the verb, plus a noun, that is the object. It must contain all the paragraph's information, without adding or removing anything. For example: Poverty causes teenage pregnancy.
slides.example: Poverty causes teenage pregnancy.

slides.html:
Leave out all colouring words — adjectives, adverbs, idioms, phrases, figures. Keep only the bare point.

Think of it asThe first answer you'd give if asked 'what happened?': 'Kotoko beat Hearts.' — the teams and the outcome, nothing more.
slides.say: Leave out all colouring words: adjectives, adverbs, idioms, phrases, and figures of speech. Keep only the bare point. Think of it like this. If someone asked what happened in a match, your first answer would be: Kotoko beat Hearts. Just the teams and the outcome, nothing more.
slides.example: Farmers do not earn enough money.

slides.html:
Paragraph idea: 'There are many reasons people leave the village for the city, but one major reason is the search for better-paying jobs that the village cannot offer.'

Summary: People move to the city to find jobs.
slides.say: Here is summarising in action. Suppose a paragraph says: There are many reasons people leave the village for the city, but one major reason is the search for better paying jobs that the village cannot offer. The summary is simply: People move to the city to find jobs.
slides.example: Young people move to the city to find work.

slides.html:
Recap: a summary is the text in short. Find each paragraph's point, and write it as a simple noun + action + noun sentence, free of colouring words, adding and removing nothing.

Well doneYou can now summarise any passage accurately.
slides.say: Recap. A summary is the text in short. Find each paragraph's point, and write it as a simple noun, action, noun sentence, free of colouring words, adding nothing, and removing nothing. Well done. You can now summarise any passage accurately.
slides.example: A summary states each main point as a simple sentence, free of colouring words, adding nothing extra.

rewrite.weak: The brave and hardworking farmers, who toil tirelessly day and night under the scorching sun, sadly do not earn enough money for their endless labour.
rewrite.hint: Summarise this in ONE simple sentence (noun + action + noun). Remove all colouring words.
rewrite.model: Farmers do not earn enough money.
rubric: Nothing added or removed in meaning

points.passage: There are several reasons why young people leave the village for the city. The most pressing of these is the desperate search for well-paying jobs, since the village offers few opportunities for steady income.
model: The point of the paragraph: young people move to the city to find jobs
It should be a simple sentence
Colouring words ('desperate', 'well-paying') are dropped
Summary: 'Young people move to the city to find work.'

points: Paragraph: 'Regular exercise, when done consistently and correctly, greatly strengthens the heart and dramatically improves overall physical health.'
frompoints.instruction: Write a ONE-sentence summary (noun + action + noun), free of colouring words.
frompoints.model: Exercise strengthens the heart.

question.q:
Summarise this paragraph in ONE simple sentence:

'Although mobile phones can sometimes be a distraction, they have, without any doubt, become an incredibly useful and powerful tool for fast, modern communication across the whole world.'
question.model: Mobile phones are useful for communication. (One simple noun + action + noun sentence, with all colouring words such as 'incredibly', 'powerful' and 'without any doubt' removed, and nothing added.)
rubric: Nothing added or left out in meaning

passages.title: Why Exercise Matters
passages.text: Regular exercise is essential for a healthy life. It strengthens the heart, allowing it to pump blood more efficiently throughout the body. Exercise also builds strong muscles and keeps the joints flexible, reducing the risk of injury. Beyond the body, physical activity lifts the mood by releasing chemicals in the brain that ease stress and sadness. People who exercise often sleep better and have more energy for their daily tasks. For all these reasons, doctors advise everyone to stay active.
passages.instruction: In ONE sentence for each, summarise the main benefits of exercise described in the passage (aim for 3-4 points, in simple sentences, with no colouring words).
passages.model: Exercise strengthens the heart. It builds strong muscles and flexible joints. It improves mood and reduces stress. It also improves sleep and energy.

passages.title: The Causes of Road Accidents
passages.text: Road accidents in our country have several causes. The most common is over-speeding, as many drivers ignore the speed limits in their hurry. Another major cause is the poor condition of the roads, which are full of potholes that throw vehicles off course. Driving while tired or drunk also leads to many crashes, since such drivers cannot react quickly to danger. Finally, faulty vehicles with worn tyres and weak brakes often break down at high speed. Addressing these causes would save many lives.
passages.instruction: Summarise the causes of road accidents in simple sentences (one point each, no colouring words).
passages.model: Over-speeding causes accidents. Poor roads cause accidents. Tired or drunk driving causes accidents. Faulty vehicles cause accidents.

passages.title: The Importance of Water
passages.text: Water is one of the most important resources on earth. Every living thing, from the smallest insect to the largest animal, depends on water to survive. In the home, water is used for drinking, cooking, washing and cleaning. Farmers need water to grow the crops that feed entire nations, and factories use it to produce countless goods. Water also generates electricity when it is stored behind great dams. Without a steady supply of clean water, human life simply could not continue.
passages.instruction: Summarise the main uses and importance of water in simple sentences, leaving out all colouring words.
passages.model: Water is needed by all living things to survive. It is used for drinking, cooking, washing and cleaning. It is used for farming and in factories. It is used to generate electricity.

passages.title: Effects of Deforestation
passages.text: The careless cutting down of forests brings serious harm. When trees are removed, the soil is no longer held in place, and heavy rains wash it away, leaving the land barren. The loss of trees also reduces rainfall, since forests help to form the clouds that bring rain. Many animals lose their homes and slowly disappear. Furthermore, with fewer trees to absorb it, harmful carbon dioxide builds up in the air and warms the planet. Deforestation therefore threatens both nature and people.
passages.instruction: Summarise the effects of deforestation, one simple sentence per effect, with no colouring words.
passages.model: Deforestation causes soil erosion. It reduces rainfall. It destroys animals' homes. It increases carbon dioxide and warms the planet.

passages.title: Benefits of Living in a Village
passages.text: Life in the village offers many quiet advantages. The air is clean and free from the smoke and noise of the city. Food is fresh, as most of it is grown on nearby farms and eaten the same day. The cost of living is low, for housing and food are cheap and plentiful. Above all, villagers enjoy a strong sense of community, where neighbours know one another and help in times of need. For those seeking peace, the village can be a restful home.
passages.instruction: Summarise the advantages of village life in simple sentences (one point each, no colouring words).
passages.model: Village air is clean. Village food is fresh. The cost of living is low. There is a strong sense of community.

passages.title: The Dangers of Junk Food
passages.text: Eating too much junk food is harmful to health. Such foods are loaded with sugar and fat, which cause people to gain unhealthy weight. They contain little of the vitamins and fibre the body needs to function well. A diet heavy in junk food can lead to serious diseases such as diabetes and heart problems. These foods also harm the teeth, causing decay and pain. For a healthy life, fresh and balanced meals are far better than junk food.
passages.instruction: Summarise the dangers of junk food in simple sentences, removing all colouring words.
passages.model: Junk food causes unhealthy weight gain. It lacks vitamins and fibre. It causes diseases such as diabetes and heart problems. It damages the teeth.

passages.title: Why Education Is Important
passages.text: Education is the key to a better future. It gives people the knowledge and skills they need to find good work and earn a living. An educated person can read important information, make wise decisions and avoid being cheated. Education also reduces poverty, for skilled workers can build businesses and create jobs for others. Beyond money, it helps people understand the world and live as responsible citizens. A well-educated nation is a strong and progressive one.
passages.instruction: Summarise the importance of education in simple sentences (3-4 points, no colouring words).
passages.model: Education provides knowledge and skills for work. It helps people make wise decisions. It reduces poverty. It makes people responsible citizens.

passages.title: Keeping the Environment Clean
passages.text: Keeping our environment clean brings many rewards. A clean surrounding prevents the spread of diseases such as cholera and malaria, which breed in filth and stagnant water. It makes a community pleasant to look at and pleasant to live in. Clean drains carry away rainwater and prevent the floods that destroy homes. A tidy environment also attracts visitors and businesses, bringing income to the area. For the good of all, everyone must help to keep the surroundings clean.
passages.instruction: Summarise the benefits of keeping the environment clean, one simple sentence per point, no colouring words.
passages.model: A clean environment prevents disease. It makes a community pleasant. It prevents floods. It attracts visitors and businesses.

passages.title: The Uses of the Mobile Phone
passages.text: The mobile phone has become a useful tool in modern life. Its first purpose is communication, allowing people to call and message family and friends anywhere in the world. It is also used to send and receive money quickly through mobile banking. Students use it to search for information and learn online. The phone serves as a camera, a clock, a torch and a source of entertainment as well. When used wisely, it makes daily life easier in countless ways.
passages.instruction: Summarise the uses of the mobile phone in simple sentences (one use per point, no colouring words).
passages.model: The phone is used for communication. It is used to send and receive money. It is used to find information and learn. It serves as a camera, clock, torch and source of entertainment.

passages.title: Why We Should Save Money
passages.text: Saving money is a wise habit for everyone. Money set aside provides security in times of emergency, such as illness or the loss of a job. Savings allow a person to buy valuable things, like land or a house, without falling into heavy debt. They also provide the capital needed to start a business and become independent. A person who saves regularly worries less about the future. For a secure life, the habit of saving should begin early.
passages.instruction: Summarise the reasons for saving money in simple sentences, leaving out all colouring words.
passages.model: Saving gives security in emergencies. It lets people buy valuable things without debt. It provides capital to start a business. It reduces worry about the future.
